# Watchlist | Advanced Charts Documentation

[Skip to main content](https://www.tradingview.com/charting-library-docs/v27/trading_terminal/Watch-List/#__docusaurus_skipToContent_fallback)

This is documentation for **v27**, which is no longer actively maintained.

For up-to-date documentation, see the **[latest version](https://www.tradingview.com/charting-library-docs/)
**.

On this page

Overview[​](https://www.tradingview.com/charting-library-docs/v27/trading_terminal/Watch-List/#overview "Direct link to Overview")

-----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

The **Watchlist** is a widget that allows users to track price movements and volume of specific financial instruments in real-time. Watchlists also allow users to quickly switch between the symbols. The Watchlist widget is displayed on the widget panel on the right side of the chart.

![Watchlist widget](https://www.tradingview.com/charting-library-docs/v27/assets/images/watchlist-165bfd72ff76eb3666c7c5c295071e2a.png)

Users can manage their lists through the UI. For example, they can create new lists, modify existing ones, export active lists, and perform other actions. You can also programmatically manage user lists using the [Watchlist API](https://www.tradingview.com/charting-library-docs/v27/trading_terminal/Watch-List/#watchlist-api)
.

![Watchlist widget menu](https://www.tradingview.com/charting-library-docs/v27/assets/images/watchlist-menu-4ef54c21a79245e381e56bfac01ebc86.png)

Enable the widget[​](https://www.tradingview.com/charting-library-docs/v27/trading_terminal/Watch-List/#enable-the-widget "Direct link to Enable the widget")

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

info

The Watchlist widget requires quote data. Therefore, you need to implement the [`getQuotes`](https://www.tradingview.com/charting-library-docs/v27/connecting_data/Datafeed-API#getquotes)
, [`subscribeQuotes`](https://www.tradingview.com/charting-library-docs/v27/connecting_data/Datafeed-API#subscribequotes)
, and [`unsubscribeQuotes`](https://www.tradingview.com/charting-library-docs/v27/connecting_data/Datafeed-API#unsubscribequotes)
 methods within your Datafeed API.

The [`widgetbar`](https://www.tradingview.com/charting-library-docs/v27/api/interfaces/Charting_Library.TradingTerminalWidgetOptions#widgetbar)
 property of the [Widget Constructor](https://www.tradingview.com/charting-library-docs/v27/core_concepts/Widget-Constructor)
 allows managing the widget panel, including the Watchlist widget. To enable the widget, set the [`watchlist`](https://www.tradingview.com/charting-library-docs/v27/api/interfaces/Charting_Library.WidgetBarParams#watchlist)
 property to `true`. You can also select the default symbols for the default watchlist and make it read-only using the [`watchlist_settings`](https://www.tradingview.com/charting-library-docs/v27/api/interfaces/Charting_Library.WidgetBarParams#watchlist_settings)
 property.

    const datafeed = new Datafeeds.UDFCompatibleDatafeed("https://demo-feed-data.tradingview.com");new TradingView.widget({    container: "chartContainer",    locale: "en",    library_path: "charting_library/",    datafeed: datafeed,    symbol: "AAPL",    interval: "1D",    widgetbar: {        watchlist: true,        watchlist_settings: {            default_symbols: ["AAPL", "IBM", "MSFT"],            readonly: true,        },    },})

Watchlist API[​](https://www.tradingview.com/charting-library-docs/v27/trading_terminal/Watch-List/#watchlist-api "Direct link to Watchlist API")

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

The Watchlist API provides extensive ways to interact with watchlists. For example, you can modify watchlist values on the fly or subscribe to events to keep track of user watchlists.

Use the [`watchList`](https://www.tradingview.com/charting-library-docs/v27/api/interfaces/Charting_Library.IChartingLibraryWidget#watchlist)
 method to retrieve the [`IWatchListApi`](https://www.tradingview.com/charting-library-docs/v27/api/interfaces/Charting_Library.IWatchListApi)
 object for interacting with the watchlist.

    const watchlistApi = await widget.watchList();  // Get the Watchlist API

Watchlist items should be symbol names that your datafeed [`resolveSymbol`](https://www.tradingview.com/charting-library-docs/v27/connecting_data/Datafeed-API#resolvesymbol)
 method can resolve. This means that generally shorter names such as `AAPL` can be used if your datafeed understands it. However, it is recommended that you provide the symbol names as they appear within the [`LibrarySymbolInfo`](https://www.tradingview.com/charting-library-docs/v27/api/interfaces/Charting_Library.LibrarySymbolInfo)
 object, for example, `NASDAQ:AAPL`.

### Create multiple watchlists[​](https://www.tradingview.com/charting-library-docs/v27/trading_terminal/Watch-List/#create-multiple-watchlists "Direct link to Create multiple watchlists")

You cannot instantiate the widget with multiple watchlists directly from the Widget Constructor. Instead, you should use the API to create multiple watchlists. The code sample below shows how to create lists with and without specific items.

    const watchlistApi = await widget.watchList(); // Get the Watchlist APIconst firstList = watchlistApi.createList("First list"); // Create a new empty listconst secondList = watchlistApi.createList("Second list", ["AMZN", "ADBE"]); // Create another list with items

### Retrieve watchlist data[​](https://www.tradingview.com/charting-library-docs/v27/trading_terminal/Watch-List/#retrieve-watchlist-data "Direct link to Retrieve watchlist data")

Use the [`getAllLists`](https://www.tradingview.com/charting-library-docs/v27/api/interfaces/Charting_Library.IWatchListApi#getalllists)
 method to get all watchlists' data such as their IDs, titles, and symbol IDs.

    const watchlistApi = await widget.watchList(); // Get the Watchlist APIwatchlistApi.getAllLists();

This method returns an instance of the `WatchListSymbolListMap` interface that contains watchlists' IDs and the corresponding [`WatchListSymbolList`](https://www.tradingview.com/charting-library-docs/v27/api/interfaces/Charting_Library.WatchListSymbolList)
 objects.

    {   "id123456789": {      "id": "id123456789",      "symbols": [         "###TOP SECTION",         "AAPL",         "IBM",         "###SECOND SECTION",         "MSFT",         "###NEW SECTION",         "AMZN"      ],      "title": "Watchlist"   },   "id987654321": {      "id": "id987654321",      "symbols": [         "MSFT",         "ADBE",         "AMZN"      ],      "title": "My list"   }}

You can also call the [`getActiveListId`](https://www.tradingview.com/charting-library-docs/v27/api/interfaces/Charting_Library.IWatchListApi#getactivelistid)
 method to get an ID of the active watchlist.

Add sections[​](https://www.tradingview.com/charting-library-docs/v27/trading_terminal/Watch-List/#add-sections "Direct link to Add sections")

-----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

The more symbols users add, the more difficult it becomes to manage and navigate the list. Sections in watchlists help users organize their lists more effectively. Sections can be named, moved, and deleted.

You can also programmatically add sections to watchlists using sections dividers. Any list item that has the `###` prefix is considered a section divider.

*   Adding sections to the default watchlist within the [Widget Constructor](https://www.tradingview.com/charting-library-docs/v27/trading_terminal/Watch-List/#enable-the-widget)
    .
    
        widgetbar: {    watchlist: true,    watchlist_settings: {        default_symbols: ["###TOP SECTION", "AAPL", "IBM", "###SECOND SECTION", "MSFT"],        readonly: true,    },},
    
*   Adding sections with items to a new and existing watchlist using [API](https://www.tradingview.com/charting-library-docs/v27/trading_terminal/Watch-List/#watchlist-api)
    .
    
        // Create new watchlist with a section and itemsconst watchlistApi = await widget.watchList();const mySymbolList = watchlistApi.createList("My new list", ["###TEST SECTION", "AMZN", "ADBE"]);// Add new section and item to the active watchlistconst activeListId = watchlistApi.getActiveListId();const activeListItems = watchlistApi.getList(activeListId);watchlistApi.updateList(activeListId, [...activeListItems, "###NEW TEST SECTION", "AAPL", "MSFT"]);
    

Store watchlist items[​](https://www.tradingview.com/charting-library-docs/v27/trading_terminal/Watch-List/#store-watchlist-items "Direct link to Store watchlist items")

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Watchlist items are saved as part of the [user settings](https://www.tradingview.com/charting-library-docs/v27/saving_loading/user-settings)
. By default, user settings are saved into the browser's [`localStorage`](https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/API/Window/localStorage)
, but you can also save them using [`settings_adapter`](https://www.tradingview.com/charting-library-docs/v27/saving_loading/user-settings#settings-adapter)
 in your preferred storage.

If you want to store watchlist items separately from other settings, you can implement your logic to store the watchlist data on your server. You can use the `IWatchListApi` methods such as [`onListAdded`](https://www.tradingview.com/charting-library-docs/v27/api/interfaces/Charting_Library.IWatchListApi#onlistadded)
, [`onListChanged`](https://www.tradingview.com/charting-library-docs/v27/api/interfaces/Charting_Library.IWatchListApi#onlistchanged)
, [`onListRemoved`](https://www.tradingview.com/charting-library-docs/v27/api/interfaces/Charting_Library.IWatchListApi#onlistremoved)
, and [`onListRenamed`](https://www.tradingview.com/charting-library-docs/v27/api/interfaces/Charting_Library.IWatchListApi#onlistrenamed)
 to know when something has changed and you should save it.

Display logos[​](https://www.tradingview.com/charting-library-docs/v27/trading_terminal/Watch-List/#display-logos "Direct link to Display logos")

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

If you want to display logos for symbols within the Watchlist widget, follow the steps below:

1.  Add the [`show_symbol_logos`](https://www.tradingview.com/charting-library-docs/v27/customization/Featuresets#show_symbol_logos)
     featureset to the [`enabled_features`](https://www.tradingview.com/charting-library-docs/v27/api/interfaces/Charting_Library.ChartingLibraryWidgetOptions#enabled_features)
     array.
2.  Provide URLs for symbols in the [`logo_urls`](https://www.tradingview.com/charting-library-docs/v27/api/interfaces/Charting_Library.LibrarySymbolInfo#logo_urls)
     property of the [`LibrarySymbolInfo`](https://www.tradingview.com/charting-library-docs/v27/api/interfaces/Charting_Library.LibrarySymbolInfo)
     object. Pass the object as a parameter to the callback of the [`resolveSymbol`](https://www.tradingview.com/charting-library-docs/v27/connecting_data/Datafeed-API#resolvesymbol)
     method.
3.  Implement the [`getQuotes`](https://www.tradingview.com/charting-library-docs/v27/connecting_data/Datafeed-API#getquotes)
    , [`subscribeQuotes`](https://www.tradingview.com/charting-library-docs/v27/connecting_data/Datafeed-API#subscribequotes)
    , and [`unsubscribeQuotes`](https://www.tradingview.com/charting-library-docs/v27/connecting_data/Datafeed-API#unsubscribequotes)
     methods within your Datafeed API.

*   [Overview](https://www.tradingview.com/charting-library-docs/v27/trading_terminal/Watch-List/#overview)
    
*   [Enable the widget](https://www.tradingview.com/charting-library-docs/v27/trading_terminal/Watch-List/#enable-the-widget)
    
*   [Watchlist API](https://www.tradingview.com/charting-library-docs/v27/trading_terminal/Watch-List/#watchlist-api)
    *   [Create multiple watchlists](https://www.tradingview.com/charting-library-docs/v27/trading_terminal/Watch-List/#create-multiple-watchlists)
        
    *   [Retrieve watchlist data](https://www.tradingview.com/charting-library-docs/v27/trading_terminal/Watch-List/#retrieve-watchlist-data)
        
*   [Add sections](https://www.tradingview.com/charting-library-docs/v27/trading_terminal/Watch-List/#add-sections)
    
*   [Store watchlist items](https://www.tradingview.com/charting-library-docs/v27/trading_terminal/Watch-List/#store-watchlist-items)
    
*   [Display logos](https://www.tradingview.com/charting-library-docs/v27/trading_terminal/Watch-List/#display-logos)

# Time zones | Advanced Charts Documentation

[Skip to main content](https://www.tradingview.com/charting-library-docs/v27/ui_elements/timezones/#__docusaurus_skipToContent_fallback)

This is documentation for **v27**, which is no longer actively maintained.

For up-to-date documentation, see the **[latest version](https://www.tradingview.com/charting-library-docs/)
**.

On this page

Supported time zones[​](https://www.tradingview.com/charting-library-docs/v27/ui_elements/timezones/#supported-time-zones "Direct link to Supported time zones")

-----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

We expect to get the name of the time zone in `olsondb` format. Time zone should be defined within the [`timezone`](https://www.tradingview.com/charting-library-docs/v27/api/interfaces/Charting_Library.LibrarySymbolInfo#timezone)
 property for Symbol information.

_Supported time zones are:_

*   `Etc/UTC`
*   `Africa/Cairo`
*   `Africa/Casablanca`
*   `Africa/Johannesburg`
*   `Africa/Lagos`
*   `America/Anchorage`
*   `America/Argentina/Buenos_Aires`
*   `America/Bogota`
*   `America/Caracas`
*   `America/Chicago`
*   `America/El_Salvador`
*   `America/Juneau`
*   `America/Lima`
*   `America/Los_Angeles`
*   `America/Mexico_City`
*   `America/New_York`
*   `America/Phoenix`
*   `America/Santiago`
*   `America/Sao_Paulo`
*   `America/Toronto`
*   `America/Vancouver`
*   `Asia/Almaty`
*   `Asia/Ashkhabad`
*   `Asia/Bahrain`
*   `Asia/Bangkok`
*   `Asia/Chongqing`
*   `Asia/Colombo`
*   `Asia/Dhaka`
*   `Asia/Dubai`
*   `Asia/Ho_Chi_Minh`
*   `Asia/Hong_Kong`
*   `Asia/Jakarta`
*   `Asia/Jerusalem`
*   `Asia/Karachi`
*   `Asia/Kathmandu`
*   `Asia/Kolkata`
*   `Asia/Kuwait`
*   `Asia/Manila`
*   `Asia/Muscat`
*   `Asia/Nicosia`
*   `Asia/Qatar`
*   `Asia/Riyadh`
*   `Asia/Yangon`
*   `Asia/Seoul`
*   `Asia/Shanghai`
*   `Asia/Singapore`
*   `Asia/Taipei`
*   `Asia/Tehran`
*   `Asia/Tokyo`
*   `Atlantic/Reykjavik`
*   `Australia/Adelaide`
*   `Australia/Brisbane`
*   `Australia/Perth`
*   `Australia/Sydney`
*   `Europe/Athens`
*   `Europe/Belgrade`
*   `Europe/Berlin`
*   `Europe/Bratislava`
*   `Europe/Brussels`
*   `Europe/Bucharest`
*   `Europe/Budapest`
*   `Europe/Copenhagen`
*   `Europe/Helsinki`
*   `Europe/Istanbul`
*   `Europe/London`
*   `Europe/Luxembourg`
*   `Europe/Madrid`
*   `Europe/Moscow`
*   `Europe/Oslo`
*   `Europe/Paris`
*   `Europe/Prague`
*   `Europe/Riga`
*   `Europe/Rome`
*   `Europe/Stockholm`
*   `Europe/Tallinn`
*   `Europe/Vienna`
*   `Europe/Vilnius`
*   `Europe/Warsaw`
*   `Europe/Zurich`
*   `Pacific/Auckland`
*   `Pacific/Chatham`
*   `Pacific/Fakaofo`
*   `Pacific/Honolulu`
*   `Pacific/Norfolk`
*   `US/Mountain`

If your time zone is not supported, you can request it on [GitHub Issues](https://github.com/tradingview/charting_library/issues "The repository is private.")
 🔐 (access is [restricted](https://www.tradingview.com/charting-library-docs/v27/getting_started/quick-start#getting-access "Click to open the 'Getting Access' section.")
) or add your [custom time zone](https://www.tradingview.com/charting-library-docs/v27/ui_elements/timezones/#adding-custom-time-zones)
.

Adding custom time zones[​](https://www.tradingview.com/charting-library-docs/v27/ui_elements/timezones/#adding-custom-time-zones "Direct link to Adding custom time zones")

-----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Additional custom time zones can be specified for use within the library. These time zones can be selected from the time zone menu, chart settings, and can be used by symbols for their time zone.

Custom time zones are required to be either mapped (aliased) to a supported time zone ([see list above](https://www.tradingview.com/charting-library-docs/v27/ui_elements/timezones/#supported-time-zones)
) or to a [GMT based time zone](https://www.tradingview.com/charting-library-docs/v27/ui_elements/timezones/#gmt-based-time-zones)
.

warning

You should ensure that the alias time zone correctly matches your desired time zone in all aspects (including daylight saving time dates).

Custom time zones are specified within the widget constructor options with the [`custom_timezones`](https://www.tradingview.com/charting-library-docs/v27/api/interfaces/Charting_Library.ChartingLibraryWidgetOptions#custom_timezones)
 property.

### GMT based time zones[​](https://www.tradingview.com/charting-library-docs/v27/ui_elements/timezones/#gmt-based-time-zones "Direct link to GMT based time zones")

In addition to the default supported time zones, you can also alias your custom time zone to GMT based time zone. These time zones can only be used for the `alias` property of a custom time zone.

The format of the GMT based time zone ids is as follows:

*   Starts with `Etc/GMT`
*   followed by either a `-` or `+` sign
*   the number of hours offset
*   and (optionally) the minutes after a colon.

**Examples:**

*   `Etc/GMT+0` : same as `Etc/UTC`
*   `Etc/GMT+2` : 2 hours behind GMT
*   `Etc/GMT-4` : 4 hours ahead of GMT
*   `Etc/GMT-3:21` : 3 hours and 21 minutes ahead of GMT

**Note:** In order to conform with the [POSIX](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/POSIX)
 style, the zone names have their sign reversed from the standard [ISO 8601](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/ISO_8601#Time_offsets_from_UTC)
 convention. In the "Etc/" area, zones west of GMT have a positive sign and those east have a negative sign in their name (e.g "Etc/GMT-14" is 14 hours ahead of GMT).

### Example[​](https://www.tradingview.com/charting-library-docs/v27/ui_elements/timezones/#example "Direct link to Example")

As an example, we are going to:

*   add Cape Town as a custom time zone which will be aliased to the existing time zone of Johannesburg.
*   add Nuuk as a custom time zone which will be aliased to a GMT based time zone.

    var widget new TradingView.widget({ /* ... Other constructor options */ custom_timezones: [    {      id: 'Africa/Cape_Town',      alias: 'Africa/Johannesburg',      title: 'Cape Town',    },    {      id: 'America/Nuuk',      alias: 'Etc/GMT+3',      title: 'Nuuk',    }, ],}));

*   [Supported time zones](https://www.tradingview.com/charting-library-docs/v27/ui_elements/timezones/#supported-time-zones)
    
*   [Adding custom time zones](https://www.tradingview.com/charting-library-docs/v27/ui_elements/timezones/#adding-custom-time-zones)
    *   [GMT based time zones](https://www.tradingview.com/charting-library-docs/v27/ui_elements/timezones/#gmt-based-time-zones)
        
    *   [Example](https://www.tradingview.com/charting-library-docs/v27/ui_elements/timezones/#example)

---

# News Widget | Advanced Charts Documentation

[Skip to main content](https://www.tradingview.com/charting-library-docs/v27/trading_terminal/news/#__docusaurus_skipToContent_fallback)

This is documentation for **v27**, which is no longer actively maintained.

For up-to-date documentation, see the **[latest version](https://www.tradingview.com/charting-library-docs/)
**.

info

All content on this page applies to the Trading Platform only.

You can attach to any [RSS feed](https://www.tradingview.com/charting-library-docs/v27/api/interfaces/Charting_Library.TradingTerminalWidgetOptions#rss_news_feed)
 and even select the feed depending on the type of the financial instrument.

Also, you can specify the [news provider](https://www.tradingview.com/charting-library-docs/v27/api/interfaces/Charting_Library.TradingTerminalWidgetOptions#news_provider)
 and attach to any custom news source.

---

# Constructor | Advanced Charts Documentation

[Skip to main content](https://www.tradingview.com/charting-library-docs/v28/custom_studies/custom-indicator-constructor/#__docusaurus_skipToContent_fallback)

This is documentation for **v28**, which is no longer actively maintained.

For up-to-date documentation, see the **[latest version](https://www.tradingview.com/charting-library-docs/)
**.

On this page

Overview[​](https://www.tradingview.com/charting-library-docs/v28/custom_studies/custom-indicator-constructor/#overview "Direct link to Overview")

---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

`constructor` is a required field in the [`CustomIndicator`](https://www.tradingview.com/charting-library-docs/v28/api/interfaces/Charting_Library.CustomIndicator)
 object that you should specify to create a [custom indicator](https://www.tradingview.com/charting-library-docs/v28/custom_studies/)
.

    custom_indicators_getter: function(PineJS) {    return Promise.resolve([        {            // Indicator object            // ...            constructor: {                // ...            },        }    ]);},

The field should contain an ES5 constructor function. The library applies the `new` operator to the constructor to create an instance of the custom indicator.

The constructor contains the [`main`](https://www.tradingview.com/charting-library-docs/v28/custom_studies/custom-indicator-constructor/#main)
 and [`init`](https://www.tradingview.com/charting-library-docs/v28/custom_studies/custom-indicator-constructor/#init)
 methods that accept the following arguments:

*   [`ctx`](https://www.tradingview.com/charting-library-docs/v28/api/interfaces/Charting_Library.IContext)
     — context that stores an indicator state. It includes information about the current symbol, resolution, OHLC values of the current bar, and more.
*   `inputs` — an array of input values. Elements in the array are arranged in the same order as [inputs](https://www.tradingview.com/charting-library-docs/v28/custom_studies/metainfo/#inputs)
     in the `metainfo`.

You should implement either both methods or only `main` to calculate indicator data. In your implementation, use mathematical, logical, and technical analysis functions provided in the [`PineJSStd`](https://www.tradingview.com/charting-library-docs/v28/api/interfaces/Charting_Library.PineJSStd)
 interface.

init[​](https://www.tradingview.com/charting-library-docs/v28/custom_studies/custom-indicator-constructor/#init "Direct link to init")

---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

The [`init`](https://www.tradingview.com/charting-library-docs/v28/api/interfaces/Charting_Library.LibraryPineStudy#init)
 method is **optional**. It is designed to get additional information for an indicator and allows you to initialize variables needed for your data calculations. For example, you can reset a counter with this method. The library calls `init` when the indicator should be calculated from scratch. This can occur when:

*   The indicator is added to the chart.
*   The indicator is added to a new pane.
*   The symbol is changed.
*   The resolution is changed.
*   Additional historical data is loaded.

Consider the following example. You want to display an indicator that compares values between the current symbol and a different one. In this case, the indicator requires data for another symbol. The code sample below shows how to request data for an additional symbol using the [`new_sym`](https://www.tradingview.com/charting-library-docs/v28/api/interfaces/Charting_Library.IContext#new_sym)
 method. In this case, requesting data should be done once, therefore, it is implemented within `init`.

    this.init = function(context, inputCallback) {    var symbol = "AAPL";    var period = PineJS.Std.period(this._context);    context.new_sym(symbol, period);};

main[​](https://www.tradingview.com/charting-library-docs/v28/custom_studies/custom-indicator-constructor/#main "Direct link to main")

---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

The [`main`](https://www.tradingview.com/charting-library-docs/v28/api/interfaces/Charting_Library.LibraryPineStudy#main)
 method is **required** and should contain the main [data calculations](https://www.tradingview.com/charting-library-docs/v28/custom_studies/custom-indicator-constructor/#data-calculations)
. The library calls this method for each bar of the [main series](https://www.tradingview.com/charting-library-docs/v28/getting_started/glossary#series)
.

info

If there is more than one series, the library calls `main` for each bar of an additional series first, and then for each bar of the main series.

### Data calculations[​](https://www.tradingview.com/charting-library-docs/v28/custom_studies/custom-indicator-constructor/#data-calculations "Direct link to Data calculations")

As mentioned above, the library calls `main` for each bar of the [main series](https://www.tradingview.com/charting-library-docs/v28/getting_started/glossary#series)
. On each iteration, you can get information about previous bars only. Therefore, when you calculate the tenth bar, you can access values of the bars in the \[1..9\] range. The library does not provide future bars, and you should not use them in your calculations to avoid unexpected results.

To store data for intermediate calculations, you should create variables using API methods such as [`new_var`](https://www.tradingview.com/charting-library-docs/v28/api/interfaces/Charting_Library.IContext#new_var)
 and [`new_unlimited_var`](https://www.tradingview.com/charting-library-docs/v28/api/interfaces/Charting_Library.IContext#new_unlimited_var)
. Consider the [Requesting Data for Another Ticker](https://www.tradingview.com/charting-library-docs/v28/custom_studies/Custom-Studies-Examples#requesting-data-for-another-ticker)
 example where the timestamp of the current bar is stored using `new_var`.

### Output values[​](https://www.tradingview.com/charting-library-docs/v28/custom_studies/custom-indicator-constructor/#output-values "Direct link to Output values")

As a result, the method should return an indicator value that corresponds to the current bar. An indicator is represented with [plots](https://www.tradingview.com/charting-library-docs/v28/custom_studies/metainfo/#plots)
 specified in `metainfo`. Therefore, you should provide a value for each plot. If there is only one plot, `main` should return a number, for example `10`, or an array with one element — `[10]`. Otherwise, the result is an array where elements are arranged in the same order as plots in `metainfo`. Consider the [OHLC Plots](https://www.tradingview.com/charting-library-docs/v28/custom_studies/Custom-Studies-OHLC-Plots)
 example where four plots get the corresponding open, high, low, and close values.

Values in the array can be one of the following types:

*   Numbers, for example, `[10, 20]`.
*   Objects with the `{ offset: number, value: number, }` structure that allow you to display a value several bars forward or backward from the current bar. For example, `[{ value: 10, offset: 5, }, { value: 20, offset: 10, }]`.

Example[​](https://www.tradingview.com/charting-library-docs/v28/custom_studies/custom-indicator-constructor/#example "Direct link to Example")

------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

The code sample below demonstrates the `constructor` implementation for a simple indicator that plots the close value.

    constructor: function () {    this.main = function (context, inputCallback) {        this._context = context;        this._input = inputCallback;        var symbolClose = PineJS.Std.close(this._context);        return [symbolClose];    };},

*   [Overview](https://www.tradingview.com/charting-library-docs/v28/custom_studies/custom-indicator-constructor/#overview)
    
*   [init](https://www.tradingview.com/charting-library-docs/v28/custom_studies/custom-indicator-constructor/#init)
    
*   [main](https://www.tradingview.com/charting-library-docs/v28/custom_studies/custom-indicator-constructor/#main)
    *   [Data calculations](https://www.tradingview.com/charting-library-docs/v28/custom_studies/custom-indicator-constructor/#data-calculations)
        
    *   [Output values](https://www.tradingview.com/charting-library-docs/v28/custom_studies/custom-indicator-constructor/#output-values)
        
*   [Example](https://www.tradingview.com/charting-library-docs/v28/custom_studies/custom-indicator-constructor/#example)

---

# Custom Studies Constructor | Advanced Charts Documentation

[Skip to main content](https://www.tradingview.com/charting-library-docs/v26/custom_studies/Custom-Studies-Constructor/#__docusaurus_skipToContent_fallback)

This is documentation for **v26**, which is no longer actively maintained.

For up-to-date documentation, see the **[latest version](https://www.tradingview.com/charting-library-docs/)
**.

On this page

The Custom Study Constructor is a Function Constructor in terms of ES5. The library creates an instance of a custom study by applying operator `new` to the constructor. The constructor function is a mandatory field of [custom study object](https://www.tradingview.com/charting-library-docs/v26/custom_studies/metainfo/)
. The library expects the constructor to create an instance of the study with one mandatory method - `main()` and one optional method - `init()`. Once the study is created the library calls `init` (if exists) and `main` sequentially with empty context to collect information about all `vars` (see below).

All functions in Constructor accept [PineJS utility functions](https://www.tradingview.com/charting-library-docs/v26/custom_studies/PineJS-Utility-Functions)
.

Arguments for both functions are `context` and `inputCallback`.

The `context` is an object, containing symbol info along with some useful methods to load/store symbol:

`* - required`

*   `new_sym(ticker*, period*, currencyCode, unitId)` - is used to load new ticker
*   `select_sym(i*)` - switch context to the other symbol received trough `new_sym`, where `i` is an index of the symbol (`0` for the main series)
*   `new_var(value*)` - creates an in-memory temporary storage with depth defined by first call `new_var(value).get(n)`
*   `new_unlimited_var(value*)` - creates an in-memory temporary storage with unlimited depth

The `inputCallback` is an array of input values, placed in order of `inputs` in [Metainfo](https://www.tradingview.com/charting-library-docs/v26/custom_studies/metainfo/)
.

init(context, inputCallback)[​](https://www.tradingview.com/charting-library-docs/v26/custom_studies/Custom-Studies-Constructor/#initcontext-inputcallback "Direct link to init(context, inputCallback)")

----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

The `init` method is called only once during the lifetime of the study and designed to get additional info for the study.

    this.init = function(context, inputCallback) {    var symbol = '#EQUITY';    var period = PineJS.Std.period(this._context);    context.new_sym(symbol, period);};

main(context, inputCallback)[​](https://www.tradingview.com/charting-library-docs/v26/custom_studies/Custom-Studies-Constructor/#maincontext-inputcallback "Direct link to main(context, inputCallback)")

----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

The `main` is called every time the library wants to calculate the study. Also it's called for every bar of every symbol. Thus, if you request several additional symbols inside your indicator it will increase the count of runs.

`main` should return one of the following types:

*   a single value; this is a shorthand of an array form with the only single item; example - 10 (the same as \[10\])
*   an array of:
    *   numbers - N-th number in this array is treated as value of [n-th plot](https://www.tradingview.com/charting-library-docs/v26/custom_studies/metainfo/)
        . Example: \[10, 20\].
    *   objects with the structure `{ offset: number, value: number, }`. The same as the numbers, but allows you to override the offset of every plot (note that offset of a certain plot should always be the same). Example: `[{ value: 10, offset: 5, }, { value: 20, offset: 10, }]`.

    this.main = function(ctx, inputCallback) {    this._context = ctx;    this._input = inputCallback;    var inputValue1 = this._input(0);    var inputValue2 = this._input(1);    var zigzag1 = PineJS.Std.zigzag(inputValue1 / 100, inputValue2 / 2, this._context);    var zigzag2 = PineJS.Std.zigzagbars(inputValue1 / 100, inputValue2 / 2, this._context);    return [zigzag1, zigzag2];};

*   [init(context, inputCallback)](https://www.tradingview.com/charting-library-docs/v26/custom_studies/Custom-Studies-Constructor/#initcontext-inputcallback)
    
*   [main(context, inputCallback)](https://www.tradingview.com/charting-library-docs/v26/custom_studies/Custom-Studies-Constructor/#maincontext-inputcallback)

---

# PineJS Utility Functions | Advanced Charts Documentation

[Skip to main content](https://www.tradingview.com/charting-library-docs/v28/custom_studies/PineJS-Utility-Functions/#__docusaurus_skipToContent_fallback)

This is documentation for **v28**, which is no longer actively maintained.

For up-to-date documentation, see the **[latest version](https://www.tradingview.com/charting-library-docs/)
**.

On this page

Custom Study [Constructor](https://www.tradingview.com/charting-library-docs/v28/custom_studies/custom-indicator-constructor)
 is able to use utility functions from PineJS object (an argument of [custom\_indicators\_getter](https://www.tradingview.com/charting-library-docs/v28/api/interfaces/Charting_Library.ChartingLibraryWidgetOptions#custom_indicators_getter)
 function).

Functions ported from Pine[​](https://www.tradingview.com/charting-library-docs/v28/custom_studies/PineJS-Utility-Functions/#functions-ported-from-pine "Direct link to Functions ported from Pine")

-----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

### `na(x)`[​](https://www.tradingview.com/charting-library-docs/v28/custom_studies/PineJS-Utility-Functions/#nax "Direct link to nax")

Test value if it's a NaN.

**Returns**

`true` if `x` is not a valid number (`x` is NaN), otherwise `false`.

### `nz(x, y)`[​](https://www.tradingview.com/charting-library-docs/v28/custom_studies/PineJS-Utility-Functions/#nzx-y "Direct link to nzx-y")

Replaces NaN values with zeros (or given value) in a series.

**Returns**

`x` if it's a valid (not NaN) number, otherwise y

### `and(expr1, expr2)`[​](https://www.tradingview.com/charting-library-docs/v28/custom_studies/PineJS-Utility-Functions/#andexpr1-expr2 "Direct link to andexpr1-expr2")

Logical AND. Applicable to boolean expressions.

**Returns**

Boolean value, or series of boolean values.

### `or(expr1, expr2)`[​](https://www.tradingview.com/charting-library-docs/v28/custom_studies/PineJS-Utility-Functions/#orexpr1-expr2 "Direct link to orexpr1-expr2")

Logical OR. Applicable to boolean expressions.

**Returns**

Boolean value, or series of boolean values.

### `not(expr)`[​](https://www.tradingview.com/charting-library-docs/v28/custom_studies/PineJS-Utility-Functions/#notexpr "Direct link to notexpr")

Logical negation (NOT). Applicable to boolean expressions.

**Returns**

Boolean value, or series of boolean values.

### `max(x1, x2, ..., x10)`[​](https://www.tradingview.com/charting-library-docs/v28/custom_studies/PineJS-Utility-Functions/#maxx1-x2--x10 "Direct link to maxx1-x2--x10")

**Returns**

The greatest of multiple values

### `min(x1, x2, ..., x10)`[​](https://www.tradingview.com/charting-library-docs/v28/custom_studies/PineJS-Utility-Functions/#minx1-x2--x10 "Direct link to minx1-x2--x10")

**Returns**

The smallest of multiple given values.

### `pow(base, exponent)`[​](https://www.tradingview.com/charting-library-docs/v28/custom_studies/PineJS-Utility-Functions/#powbase-exponent "Direct link to powbase-exponent")

Mathematical power function.

**Returns**

`x` raised to the power of `y`. If `x` is a series, it is calculated elementwise.

**Arguments**

*   **`base` (series\[float\])** Specify the base to use.
*   **`exponent` (float)** Specifies the exponent.

### `abs(x)`[​](https://www.tradingview.com/charting-library-docs/v28/custom_studies/PineJS-Utility-Functions/#absx "Direct link to absx")

Absolute value of `x` is `x` if `x >= 0`, or `-x` otherwise.

**Returns**

The absolute value of x

### `log(x)`[​](https://www.tradingview.com/charting-library-docs/v28/custom_studies/PineJS-Utility-Functions/#logx "Direct link to logx")

Natural logarithm of any `x > 0` is the unique `y` such that `e^y = x`

**Returns**

The natural logarithm of x.

### `log10(x)`[​](https://www.tradingview.com/charting-library-docs/v28/custom_studies/PineJS-Utility-Functions/#log10x "Direct link to log10x")

Base 10 logarithm of any `x > 0` is the unique `y` such that `10^y = x`

**Returns**

The base 10 logarithm of x.

### `sqrt(x)`[​](https://www.tradingview.com/charting-library-docs/v28/custom_studies/PineJS-Utility-Functions/#sqrtx "Direct link to sqrtx")

Square root of any `x >= 0` is the unique `y >= 0` such that `y^2 = x`

**Returns**

The square root of x.

### `sign(x)`[​](https://www.tradingview.com/charting-library-docs/v28/custom_studies/PineJS-Utility-Functions/#signx "Direct link to signx")

Sign (signum) of `x` is zero if the `x` is zero, `1.0` if the `x` is greater than zero, `-1.0` if the `x` is less than zero.

**Returns**

The sign of the argument.

### `exp(x)`[​](https://www.tradingview.com/charting-library-docs/v28/custom_studies/PineJS-Utility-Functions/#expx "Direct link to expx")

The exp function of `x` is `e^x`, where `x` is the argument and `e` is Euler's number.

**Returns**

A number representing e^x.

### `sin(x)`[​](https://www.tradingview.com/charting-library-docs/v28/custom_studies/PineJS-Utility-Functions/#sinx "Direct link to sinx")

The `sin` function returns the trigonometric sine of an angle.

**Returns**

The trigonometric sine of an angle.

**Arguments**

*   **`x` (series\[float\])** Angle, in radians.

### `cos(x)`[​](https://www.tradingview.com/charting-library-docs/v28/custom_studies/PineJS-Utility-Functions/#cosx "Direct link to cosx")

The cos function returns the trigonometric cosine of an angle.

**Returns**

The trigonometric cosine of an angle.

**Arguments**

*   **`x` (series\[float\])** Angle, in radians.

### `tan(x)`[​](https://www.tradingview.com/charting-library-docs/v28/custom_studies/PineJS-Utility-Functions/#tanx "Direct link to tanx")

The tan function returns the trigonometric tangent of an angle.

**Returns**

The trigonometric tangent of an angle.

**Arguments**

*   **`x` (series\[float\])** Angle, in radians.

### `asin(x)`[​](https://www.tradingview.com/charting-library-docs/v28/custom_studies/PineJS-Utility-Functions/#asinx "Direct link to asinx")

The asin function returns the arcsine (in radians) of number such that `sin(asin(y)) = y` for `y` in range `[-1, 1]`.

**Returns**

The arcsine of a value; the returned angle is in the range `[-Pi/2, Pi/2]`, or na if y is outside of range `[-1, 1]`.

### `acos(x)`[​](https://www.tradingview.com/charting-library-docs/v28/custom_studies/PineJS-Utility-Functions/#acosx "Direct link to acosx")

The acos function returns the arccosine (in radians) of number such that `cos(acos(y)) = y` for `y` in range `[-1, 1]`.

**Returns**

The arc cosine of a value; the returned angle is in the range `[0, Pi]`, or `na` if `y` is outside of range `[-1, 1]`.

### `atan(x)`[​](https://www.tradingview.com/charting-library-docs/v28/custom_studies/PineJS-Utility-Functions/#atanx "Direct link to atanx")

The atan function returns the arctangent (in radians) of number such that `tan(atan(y)) = y` for any `y`.

**Returns**

The arc tangent of a value; the returned angle is in the range `[-Pi/2, Pi/2]`.

### `floor(x)`[​](https://www.tradingview.com/charting-library-docs/v28/custom_studies/PineJS-Utility-Functions/#floorx "Direct link to floorx")

**Returns**

The largest integer less than or equal to the given number.

### `ceil(x)`[​](https://www.tradingview.com/charting-library-docs/v28/custom_studies/PineJS-Utility-Functions/#ceilx "Direct link to ceilx")

The ceil function returns the smallest (closest to negative infinity) integer that is greater than or equal to the argument.

**Returns**

The smallest integer greater than or equal to the given number.

### `round(x)`[​](https://www.tradingview.com/charting-library-docs/v28/custom_studies/PineJS-Utility-Functions/#roundx "Direct link to roundx")

**Returns**

The value of `x` rounded to the nearest integer, with ties rounding up. If the precision parameter is used, returns a float value rounded to that number of decimal places.

### `avg(x1, x2, ..., x10)`[​](https://www.tradingview.com/charting-library-docs/v28/custom_studies/PineJS-Utility-Functions/#avgx1-x2--x10 "Direct link to avgx1-x2--x10")

Calculates average of all given series (elementwise).

**Returns**

Average.

### `open(context)`[​](https://www.tradingview.com/charting-library-docs/v28/custom_studies/PineJS-Utility-Functions/#opencontext "Direct link to opencontext")

Current open price.

**Arguments**

*   **`context`** PineJS execution context.

### `high(context)`[​](https://www.tradingview.com/charting-library-docs/v28/custom_studies/PineJS-Utility-Functions/#highcontext "Direct link to highcontext")

Current high price.

**Arguments**

*   **`context`** PineJS execution context.

### `low(context)`[​](https://www.tradingview.com/charting-library-docs/v28/custom_studies/PineJS-Utility-Functions/#lowcontext "Direct link to lowcontext")

Current low price.

**Arguments**

*   **`context`** PineJS execution context.

### `close(context)`[​](https://www.tradingview.com/charting-library-docs/v28/custom_studies/PineJS-Utility-Functions/#closecontext "Direct link to closecontext")

Close price of the current bar when it has closed, or last traded price of a yet incomplete, realtime bar.

**Arguments**

*   **`context`** PineJS execution context.

### `hl2(context)`[​](https://www.tradingview.com/charting-library-docs/v28/custom_studies/PineJS-Utility-Functions/#hl2context "Direct link to hl2context")

Is a shortcut for `(high + low)/2`

**Arguments**

*   **`context`** PineJS execution context.

### `hlc3(context)`[​](https://www.tradingview.com/charting-library-docs/v28/custom_studies/PineJS-Utility-Functions/#hlc3context "Direct link to hlc3context")

Is a shortcut for (high + low + close)/3

**Arguments**

*   **`context`** PineJS execution context.

### `ohlc4(context)`[​](https://www.tradingview.com/charting-library-docs/v28/custom_studies/PineJS-Utility-Functions/#ohlc4context "Direct link to ohlc4context")

Is a shortcut for (open + high + low + close)/4

**Arguments**

*   **`context`** PineJS execution context.

### `volume(context)`[​](https://www.tradingview.com/charting-library-docs/v28/custom_studies/PineJS-Utility-Functions/#volumecontext "Direct link to volumecontext")

Current bar volume.

**Arguments**

*   **`context`** PineJS execution context.

### `period(context)`[​](https://www.tradingview.com/charting-library-docs/v28/custom_studies/PineJS-Utility-Functions/#periodcontext "Direct link to periodcontext")

Resolution string, e.g. `60` - 60 minutes, `D` - daily, `W` - weekly, `M` - monthly, `5D` - 5 days, `12M` - one year, `3M` - one quarter

**Arguments**

*   **`context`** PineJS execution context.

### `tickerid(context)`[​](https://www.tradingview.com/charting-library-docs/v28/custom_studies/PineJS-Utility-Functions/#tickeridcontext "Direct link to tickeridcontext")

**Arguments**

*   **`context`** PineJS execution context.

### `year(context)`[​](https://www.tradingview.com/charting-library-docs/v28/custom_studies/PineJS-Utility-Functions/#yearcontext "Direct link to yearcontext")

Current bar year in exchange timezone.

**Arguments**

*   **`context`** PineJS execution context.

### `month(context)`[​](https://www.tradingview.com/charting-library-docs/v28/custom_studies/PineJS-Utility-Functions/#monthcontext "Direct link to monthcontext")

Current bar month in exchange timezone.

**Arguments**

*   **`context`** PineJS execution context.

### `weekofyear(context)`[​](https://www.tradingview.com/charting-library-docs/v28/custom_studies/PineJS-Utility-Functions/#weekofyearcontext "Direct link to weekofyearcontext")

Week number of current bar time in exchange timezone.

**Arguments**

*   **`context`** PineJS execution context.

### `dayofmonth(context)`[​](https://www.tradingview.com/charting-library-docs/v28/custom_studies/PineJS-Utility-Functions/#dayofmonthcontext "Direct link to dayofmonthcontext")

Date of current bar time in exchange timezone.

**Arguments**

*   **`context`** PineJS execution context.

### `dayofweek(context)`[​](https://www.tradingview.com/charting-library-docs/v28/custom_studies/PineJS-Utility-Functions/#dayofweekcontext "Direct link to dayofweekcontext")

Day of week for current bar time in exchange timezone.

**Arguments**

*   **`context`** PineJS execution context.

### `hour(context)`[​](https://www.tradingview.com/charting-library-docs/v28/custom_studies/PineJS-Utility-Functions/#hourcontext "Direct link to hourcontext")

Current bar hour in exchange timezone.

**Arguments**

*   **`context`** PineJS execution context.

### `minute(context)`[​](https://www.tradingview.com/charting-library-docs/v28/custom_studies/PineJS-Utility-Functions/#minutecontext "Direct link to minutecontext")

Current bar minute in exchange timezone.

**Arguments**

*   **`context`** PineJS execution context.

### `second(context)`[​](https://www.tradingview.com/charting-library-docs/v28/custom_studies/PineJS-Utility-Functions/#secondcontext "Direct link to secondcontext")

Current bar second in exchange timezone.

**Arguments**

*   **`context`** PineJS execution context.

### `iff(condition, then, _else)`[​](https://www.tradingview.com/charting-library-docs/v28/custom_studies/PineJS-Utility-Functions/#iffcondition-then-_else "Direct link to iffcondition-then-_else")

If ... then ... else ...

**Remarks**

`iff` does exactly the same thing as ternary conditional operator `?:` but in a functional style. Also iff is slightly less efficient than operator `?:`

### `rising(series, length)`[​](https://www.tradingview.com/charting-library-docs/v28/custom_studies/PineJS-Utility-Functions/#risingseries-length "Direct link to risingseries-length")

Test if the `series` is now rising for `length` bars long.

**Returns**

`true` if current `x` is greater than any previous `x` for `length` bars back, `false` otherwise.

**Arguments**

*   **`series` (series\[float\])** Series of values to process.
*   **`length` (integer)** Number of bars (length).

### `falling(series, length)`[​](https://www.tradingview.com/charting-library-docs/v28/custom_studies/PineJS-Utility-Functions/#fallingseries-length "Direct link to fallingseries-length")

Test if the `series` is now falling for `length` bars long.

**Returns**

`true` if current `x` is less than any previous `x` for `length` bars back, `false` otherwise.

**Arguments**

*   **`series` (series\[float\])** Series of values to process.
*   **`length` (integer)** Number of bars (length).

### `rsi(upper, lower)`[​](https://www.tradingview.com/charting-library-docs/v28/custom_studies/PineJS-Utility-Functions/#rsiupper-lower "Direct link to rsiupper-lower")

Relative strength index. It is calculated based on rma's of upward and downward change of x.

**Returns**

Relative strength index.

**Arguments**

*   **`upper` (float)** Series of values to process.
*   **`lower` (float)** Number of bars (length).

### `sum(source, length, context)`[​](https://www.tradingview.com/charting-library-docs/v28/custom_studies/PineJS-Utility-Functions/#sumsource-length-context "Direct link to sumsource-length-context")

The sum function returns the sliding sum of last y values of x.

**Returns**

Sum of x for y bars back.

**Arguments**

*   **`source` (series\[float\])** Series of values to process.
*   **`length` (integer)** Number of bars (length).
*   **`context`** PineJS execution context.

### `sma(source, length, context)`[​](https://www.tradingview.com/charting-library-docs/v28/custom_studies/PineJS-Utility-Functions/#smasource-length-context "Direct link to smasource-length-context")

The sma function returns the moving average, that is the sum of last `length` values of `source`, divided by `length`.

**Returns**

Simple moving average of x for y bars back.

**Arguments**

*   **`source` (series\[float\])** Series of values to process.
*   **`length` (integer)** Number of bars (length).
*   **`context`** PineJS execution context.

### `rma(source, length, context)`[​](https://www.tradingview.com/charting-library-docs/v28/custom_studies/PineJS-Utility-Functions/#rmasource-length-context "Direct link to rmasource-length-context")

Moving average used in RSI. It is the exponentially weighted moving average with alpha = 1 / length.

**Returns**

Exponential moving average of x with alpha = 1 / y.

**Arguments**

*   **`source` (series\[float\])** Series of values to process.
*   **`length` (integer)** Number of bars (length).
*   **`context`** PineJS execution context.

### `fixnan(current, context)`[​](https://www.tradingview.com/charting-library-docs/v28/custom_studies/PineJS-Utility-Functions/#fixnancurrent-context "Direct link to fixnancurrent-context")

For a given series replaces NaN values with previous nearest non-NaN value.

**Returns**

Series without `na` gaps.

**Arguments**

*   **`current` (series\[float\])** Series of values to process.
*   **`context`** PineJS execution context.

### `tr(handle_nan, context)`[​](https://www.tradingview.com/charting-library-docs/v28/custom_studies/PineJS-Utility-Functions/#trhandle_nan-context "Direct link to trhandle_nan-context")

**Returns**

True range. It is `max(high - low, abs(high - close[1]), abs(low - close[1]))`

**Arguments**

*   **`handle_nan` (bool)** How NaN values are handled. if `true`, and previous day's close is NaN then `tr` would be calculated as current day high-low. Otherwise (if `false`) `tr` would return NaN in such cases. Also note, that atr uses `tr(true)`.
*   **`context`** PineJS execution context.

### `atr(length, context)`[​](https://www.tradingview.com/charting-library-docs/v28/custom_studies/PineJS-Utility-Functions/#atrlength-context "Direct link to atrlength-context")

Function atr (average true range) returns the RMA of true range. True range is `max(high - low, abs(high - close[1]), abs(low - close[1]))`

**Returns**

Average true range.

**Arguments**

*   **`length` (integer)** Length (number of bars back).
*   **`context`** PineJS execution context.

### `ema(series, length, context)`[​](https://www.tradingview.com/charting-library-docs/v28/custom_studies/PineJS-Utility-Functions/#emaseries-length-context "Direct link to emaseries-length-context")

The ema function returns the exponentially weighted moving average. In ema weighting factors decrease exponentially. It calculates by using a formula: `EMA = alpha * x + (1 - alpha) * EMA[1]`, where `alpha = 2 / (y + 1)`

**Returns**

Exponential moving average of x with alpha = 2 / (y + 1)

**Arguments**

*   **`source` (series\[float\])** Series of values to process.
*   **`length` (integer)** Number of bars (length).
*   **`context`** PineJS execution context.

### `wma(source, length, context)`[​](https://www.tradingview.com/charting-library-docs/v28/custom_studies/PineJS-Utility-Functions/#wmasource-length-context "Direct link to wmasource-length-context")

The wma function returns weighted moving average of `source` for `length` bars back. In wma weighting factors decrease in arithmetical progression.

**Returns**

Weighted moving average of `series` for `length` bars back.

**Arguments**

*   **`source` (series\[float\])** Series of values to process.
*   **`length` (integer)** Number of bars (length).
*   **`context`** PineJS execution context.

### `vwma(source, length, context)`[​](https://www.tradingview.com/charting-library-docs/v28/custom_studies/PineJS-Utility-Functions/#vwmasource-length-context "Direct link to vwmasource-length-context")

The `vwma` function returns volume-weighted moving average of `source` for `length` bars back. It is the same as: `sma(x * volume, y) / sma(volume, y)`

**Returns**

Volume-weighted moving average of `source` for `length` bars back.

**Arguments**

*   **`source` (series\[float\])** Series of values to process.
*   **`length` (integer)** Number of bars (length).
*   **`context`** PineJS execution context.

### `swma(source, context)`[​](https://www.tradingview.com/charting-library-docs/v28/custom_studies/PineJS-Utility-Functions/#swmasource-context "Direct link to swmasource-context")

Symmetrically weighted moving average with fixed length: 4. Weights: `[1/6, 2/6, 2/6, 1/6]`.

**Returns**

Symmetrically weighted moving average

**Arguments**

*   **`source` (series\[float\])** Source series.
*   **`context`** PineJS execution context.

### `lowestbars(source, length, context)`[​](https://www.tradingview.com/charting-library-docs/v28/custom_studies/PineJS-Utility-Functions/#lowestbarssource-length-context "Direct link to lowestbarssource-length-context")

Lowest value offset for a given number of bars back.

**Returns**

Offset to the lowest bar.

**Arguments**

*   **`source` (series\[float\])** Series of values to process.
*   **`length` (integer)** Number of bars (length).
*   **`context`** PineJS execution context.

### `lowest(source, length, context)`[​](https://www.tradingview.com/charting-library-docs/v28/custom_studies/PineJS-Utility-Functions/#lowestsource-length-context "Direct link to lowestsource-length-context")

Lowest value for a given number of bars back.

**Returns**

Lowest value.

**Arguments**

*   **`source` (series\[float\])** Series of values to process.
*   **`length` (integer)** Number of bars (length).
*   **`context`** PineJS execution context.

### `highestbars(source, length, context)`[​](https://www.tradingview.com/charting-library-docs/v28/custom_studies/PineJS-Utility-Functions/#highestbarssource-length-context "Direct link to highestbarssource-length-context")

Highest value offset for a given number of bars back.

**Returns**

Offset to the highest bar.

**Arguments**

*   **`source` (series\[float\])** Series of values to process.
*   **`length` (integer)** Number of bars (length).
*   **`context`** PineJS execution context.

### `highest(source, length, context)`[​](https://www.tradingview.com/charting-library-docs/v28/custom_studies/PineJS-Utility-Functions/#highestsource-length-context "Direct link to highestsource-length-context")

Highest value for a given number of bars back.

**Returns**

Highest value.

**Arguments**

*   **`source` (series\[float\])** Series of values to process.
*   **`length` (integer)** Number of bars (length).
*   **`context`** PineJS execution context.

### `cum(x, context)`[​](https://www.tradingview.com/charting-library-docs/v28/custom_studies/PineJS-Utility-Functions/#cumx-context "Direct link to cumx-context")

Cumulative (total) sum of `x`. In other words it's a sum of all elements of `x`.

**Returns**

Total sum series.

**Arguments**

*   **`x` (series\[float\])** Series of values to process.
*   **`context`** PineJS execution context.

### `accdist(context)`[​](https://www.tradingview.com/charting-library-docs/v28/custom_studies/PineJS-Utility-Functions/#accdistcontext "Direct link to accdistcontext")

Accumulation/distribution index.

**Arguments**

*   **`context`** PineJS execution context.

### `correlation(source_a, source_b, length, context)`[​](https://www.tradingview.com/charting-library-docs/v28/custom_studies/PineJS-Utility-Functions/#correlationsource_a-source_b-length-context "Direct link to correlationsource_a-source_b-length-context")

Correlation coefficient. Describes the degree to which two series tend to deviate from their `sma` values.

**Returns**

Correlation coefficient.

**Arguments**

*   **`source_a` (series\[float\])** Source series.
*   **`source_b` (series\[float\])** Target series.
*   **`length` (integer)** Length (number of bars back).
*   **`context`** PineJS execution context.

### `stoch(source, high, low, length, context)`[​](https://www.tradingview.com/charting-library-docs/v28/custom_studies/PineJS-Utility-Functions/#stochsource-high-low-length-context "Direct link to stochsource-high-low-length-context")

Stochastic. It is calculated by a formula: `100 * (close - lowest(low, length)) / (highest(high, length) - lowest(low, length))`

**Returns**

Stochastic.

**Arguments**

*   **`source` (series\[float\])** Source series.
*   **`high` (series\[float\])** Series of high.
*   **`low` (series\[float\])** Series of low.
*   **`length` (integer)** Length (number of bars back).
*   **`context`** PineJS execution context.

### `tsi(source, short_length, long_length, context)`[​](https://www.tradingview.com/charting-library-docs/v28/custom_studies/PineJS-Utility-Functions/#tsisource-short_length-long_length-context "Direct link to tsisource-short_length-long_length-context")

True strength index. It uses moving averages of the underlying momentum of a financial instrument.

**Returns**

True strength index. A value in range \[-1, 1\]

**Arguments**

*   **`source` (series\[float\])** Source series.
*   **`short_length` (integer)** Short length.
*   **`long_length` (integer)** Long length.
*   **`context`** PineJS execution context.

### `cross(x, y, context)`[​](https://www.tradingview.com/charting-library-docs/v28/custom_studies/PineJS-Utility-Functions/#crossx-y-context "Direct link to crossx-y-context")

**Returns**

`true` if two series have crossed each other, otherwise `false`.

**Arguments**

*   **`x` (series\[float\])** First series.
*   **`y` (series\[float\])** Second series.
*   **`context`** PineJS execution context.

### `linreg(source, length, offset)`[​](https://www.tradingview.com/charting-library-docs/v28/custom_studies/PineJS-Utility-Functions/#linregsource-length-offset "Direct link to linregsource-length-offset")

Linear regression curve. A line that best fits the prices specified over a user-defined time period. It is calculated using the least squares method. The result of this function is calculated using the formula: `linreg = intercept + slope * (length - 1 - offset)`, where intercept and slope are the values calculated with the least squares method on source series (x argument).

**Returns**

Linear regression curve.

**Arguments**

*   **`source` (series\[float\])** Source series.
*   **`length` (integer)**
*   **`offset` (integer)** Offset.

### `sar(start, inc, max, context)`[​](https://www.tradingview.com/charting-library-docs/v28/custom_studies/PineJS-Utility-Functions/#sarstart-inc-max-context "Direct link to sarstart-inc-max-context")

Parabolic SAR (parabolic stop and reverse) is a method devised by J. Welles Wilder, Jr., to find potential reversals in the market price direction of traded goods.

**Returns**

Parabolic SAR.

**Arguments**

*   **`start` (series\[float\])** Start.
*   **`inc` (integer)** Increment.
*   **`max` (integer)** Maximum.
*   **`context`** PineJS execution context.

### `alma(series, length, offset, sigma)`[​](https://www.tradingview.com/charting-library-docs/v28/custom_studies/PineJS-Utility-Functions/#almaseries-length-offset-sigma "Direct link to almaseries-length-offset-sigma")

Arnaud Legoux Moving Average. It uses Gaussian distribution as weights for moving average.

**Returns**

Arnaud Legoux Moving Average.

**Arguments**

*   **`series` (series\[float\])** Series of values to process.
*   **`length` (integer)** Number of bars (length).
*   **`offset` (float)** Controls tradeoff between smoothness (closer to 1) and responsiveness (closer to 0).
*   **`sigma` (float)** Changes the smoothness of ALMA. The larger sigma the smoother ALMA.

### `change(source)`[​](https://www.tradingview.com/charting-library-docs/v28/custom_studies/PineJS-Utility-Functions/#changesource "Direct link to changesource")

Difference between current value and previous, `x - x[1]`.

**Returns**

The result of subtraction.

**Arguments**

*   **`source` (series\[float\])**

### `roc(source, length)`[​](https://www.tradingview.com/charting-library-docs/v28/custom_studies/PineJS-Utility-Functions/#rocsource-length "Direct link to rocsource-length")

Function roc (rate of change) showing the difference between current value of `source` and the value of `source` that was `length` days ago. It is calculated by the formula: `100 * change(src, length) / src[length]`.

**Returns**

The rate of change of `source` for `length` bars back.

**Arguments**

*   **`source` (series\[float\])** Series of values to process.
*   **`length` (integer)** Number of bars (length).

### `dev(source, length, context)`[​](https://www.tradingview.com/charting-library-docs/v28/custom_studies/PineJS-Utility-Functions/#devsource-length-context "Direct link to devsource-length-context")

Measure of difference between the series and it's `sma`.

**Returns**

Deviation of `source` for `length` bars back.

**Arguments**

*   **`source` (series\[float\])** Series of values to process.
*   **`length` (integer)** Number of bars (length).
*   **`context`** PineJS execution context.

### `stdev(source, length, context)`[​](https://www.tradingview.com/charting-library-docs/v28/custom_studies/PineJS-Utility-Functions/#stdevsource-length-context "Direct link to stdevsource-length-context")

**Returns**

Standard deviation.

**Arguments**

*   **`source` (series\[float\])** Series of values to process.
*   **`length` (integer)** Number of bars (length).
*   **`context`** PineJS execution context.

**Remarks**

This is a biased estimation of standard deviation.

### `variance(source, length, context)`[​](https://www.tradingview.com/charting-library-docs/v28/custom_studies/PineJS-Utility-Functions/#variancesource-length-context "Direct link to variancesource-length-context")

Variance is the expectation of the squared deviation of a series from its mean `sma`, and it informally measures how far a set of numbers are spread out from their mean.

**Returns**

Variance of `source` for `length` bars back.

**Arguments**

*   **`source` (series\[float\])** Series of values to process.
*   **`length` (integer)** Number of bars (length).
*   **`context`** PineJS execution context.

**Remarks**

This is a biased estimation of sample variance.

### `percentrank(source, length)`[​](https://www.tradingview.com/charting-library-docs/v28/custom_studies/PineJS-Utility-Functions/#percentranksource-length "Direct link to percentranksource-length")

Percent rank is the percents of how many previous values was less than or equal to the current value of given series.

**Returns**

Percent rank of `source` for `length` bars back.

**Arguments**

*   **`source` (series\[float\])** Series of values to process.
*   **`length` (integer)** Number of bars (length).

### `dmi(diLength, adxSmoothingLength, context)`[​](https://www.tradingview.com/charting-library-docs/v28/custom_studies/PineJS-Utility-Functions/#dmidilength-adxsmoothinglength-context "Direct link to dmidilength-adxsmoothinglength-context")

Calculates the directional movement values +DI, -DI, DX, ADX, and ADXR.

**Returns**

An array of the +DI, -DI, DX, ADX, and ADXR values with `diLength` smoothing for the (+/-)DI values and `adxSmoothingLength` for the ADX value.

**Arguments**

*   **`diLength` (integer)** Number of bars (length) used when calculating the +DI and -DI values.
*   **`adxSmoothingLength` (integer)** Number of bars (length) used when calculating the ADX value.
*   **`context`** PineJS execution context.

Functions specific to the library[​](https://www.tradingview.com/charting-library-docs/v28/custom_studies/PineJS-Utility-Functions/#functions-specific-to-the-library "Direct link to Functions specific to the library")

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

*   `isZero(number)` - boolean, shows whether the `number` equals zero or almost zero
*   `toBool(number)` - boolean, `true` if a number is finite and not equals zero
*   `eq(arg1, arg2)` - boolean, `true` if argument numbers are equal
*   `neq(arg1, arg2)` - boolean, `true` if argument numbers are not equal
*   `ge(arg1, arg2)` - boolean, `true` if first argument number is greater or equal to second
*   `gt(arg1, arg2)` - boolean, `true` if first argument number is greater than second
*   `le(arg1, arg2)` - boolean, `true` if first argument number is lesser or equal to second
*   `lt(arg1, arg2)` - boolean, `true` if first argument is lesser than second
*   `updatetime(context)` - string, symbol update time
*   `ticker(context)` - string, symbol name without exchange prefix, e.g. 'MSFT'
*   `interval(context)` - returns symbol interval string
*   `isdwm(context)` - boolean, returns `true` if current resolution is a daily or weekly or monthly resolution
*   `isintraday(context)` - boolean, returns true if current resolution is an intraday (minutes or seconds) resolution
*   `isdaily(context)` - boolean, returns `true` if current resolution is a daily resolution
*   `isweekly(context)` - boolean, returns `true` if current resolution is a weekly resolution
*   `ismonthly(context)` - boolean, returns `true` if current resolution is a monthly resolution
*   `add_days_considering_dst(timezone, utcTime, daysCount)`
*   `selectSessionBreaks(context, times)` - select session breaks for intraday resolutions only
*   `timepart(symbol, field, time)`
*   `wvap(source, context)`
*   `createNewSessionCheck(context)`
*   `error(message)` - returns study error with `message` specified
*   `time(context, period)` - returns UNIX time of current bar

*   [Functions ported from Pine](https://www.tradingview.com/charting-library-docs/v28/custom_studies/PineJS-Utility-Functions/#functions-ported-from-pine)
    *   [`na(x)`](https://www.tradingview.com/charting-library-docs/v28/custom_studies/PineJS-Utility-Functions/#nax)

    *   [`open(context)`](https://www.tradingview.com/charting-library-docs/v28/custom_studies/PineJS-Utility-Functions/#opencontext)
        
    *   [`high(context)`](https://www.tradingview.com/charting-library-docs/v28/custom_studies/PineJS-Utility-Functions/#highcontext)
        
    *   [`low(context)`](https://www.tradingview.com/charting-library-docs/v28/custom_studies/PineJS-Utility-Functions/#lowcontext)

    *   [`hl2(context)`](https://www.tradingview.com/charting-library-docs/v28/custom_studies/PineJS-Utility-Functions/#hl2context)

    *   [`volume(context)`](https://www.tradingview.com/charting-library-docs/v28/custom_studies/PineJS-Utility-Functions/#volumecontext)
        
    *   [`period(context)`](https://www.tradingview.com/charting-library-docs/v28/custom_studies/PineJS-Utility-Functions/#periodcontext)
        
    *   [`tickerid(context)`](https://www.tradingview.com/charting-library-docs/v28/custom_studies/PineJS-Utility-Functions/#tickeridcontext)
        
    *   [`year(context)`](https://www.tradingview.com/charting-library-docs/v28/custom_studies/PineJS-Utility-Functions/#yearcontext)
        
    *   [`month(context)`](https://www.tradingview.com/charting-library-docs/v28/custom_studies/PineJS-Utility-Functions/#monthcontext)
        
    *   [`weekofyear(context)`](https://www.tradingview.com/charting-library-docs/v28/custom_studies/PineJS-Utility-Functions/#weekofyearcontext)
        
    *   [`dayofmonth(context)`](https://www.tradingview.com/charting-library-docs/v28/custom_studies/PineJS-Utility-Functions/#dayofmonthcontext)
        
    *   [`dayofweek(context)`](https://www.tradingview.com/charting-library-docs/v28/custom_studies/PineJS-Utility-Functions/#dayofweekcontext)
        
    *   [`hour(context)`](https://www.tradingview.com/charting-library-docs/v28/custom_studies/PineJS-Utility-Functions/#hourcontext)
        
    *   [`minute(context)`](https://www.tradingview.com/charting-library-docs/v28/custom_studies/PineJS-Utility-Functions/#minutecontext)
        
    *   [`second(context)`](https://www.tradingview.com/charting-library-docs/v28/custom_studies/PineJS-Utility-Functions/#secondcontext)

    *   [`atr(length, context)`](https://www.tradingview.com/charting-library-docs/v28/custom_studies/PineJS-Utility-Functions/#atrlength-context)
        
    *   [`ema(series, length, context)`](https://www.tradingview.com/charting-library-docs/v28/custom_studies/PineJS-Utility-Functions/#emaseries-length-context)

    *   [`vwma(source, length, context)`](https://www.tradingview.com/charting-library-docs/v28/custom_studies/PineJS-Utility-Functions/#vwmasource-length-context)
        
    *   [`swma(source, context)`](https://www.tradingview.com/charting-library-docs/v28/custom_studies/PineJS-Utility-Functions/#swmasource-context)
        
    *   [`lowestbars(source, length, context)`](https://www.tradingview.com/charting-library-docs/v28/custom_studies/PineJS-Utility-Functions/#lowestbarssource-length-context)
        
    *   [`lowest(source, length, context)`](https://www.tradingview.com/charting-library-docs/v28/custom_studies/PineJS-Utility-Functions/#lowestsource-length-context)
        
    *   [`highestbars(source, length, context)`](https://www.tradingview.com/charting-library-docs/v28/custom_studies/PineJS-Utility-Functions/#highestbarssource-length-context)
        
    *   [`highest(source, length, context)`](https://www.tradingview.com/charting-library-docs/v28/custom_studies/PineJS-Utility-Functions/#highestsource-length-context)
        
    *   [`cum(x, context)`](https://www.tradingview.com/charting-library-docs/v28/custom_studies/PineJS-Utility-Functions/#cumx-context)
        
    *   [`accdist(context)`](https://www.tradingview.com/charting-library-docs/v28/custom_studies/PineJS-Utility-Functions/#accdistcontext)
        
    *   [`correlation(source_a, source_b, length, context)`](https://www.tradingview.com/charting-library-docs/v28/custom_studies/PineJS-Utility-Functions/#correlationsource_a-source_b-length-context)
        
    *   [`stoch(source, high, low, length, context)`](https://www.tradingview.com/charting-library-docs/v28/custom_studies/PineJS-Utility-Functions/#stochsource-high-low-length-context)
        
    *   [`tsi(source, short_length, long_length, context)`](https://www.tradingview.com/charting-library-docs/v28/custom_studies/PineJS-Utility-Functions/#tsisource-short_length-long_length-context)

    *   [`sar(start, inc, max, context)`](https://www.tradingview.com/charting-library-docs/v28/custom_studies/PineJS-Utility-Functions/#sarstart-inc-max-context)
        
    *   [`alma(series, length, offset, sigma)`](https://www.tradingview.com/charting-library-docs/v28/custom_studies/PineJS-Utility-Functions/#almaseries-length-offset-sigma)
        
    *   [`change(source)`](https://www.tradingview.com/charting-library-docs/v28/custom_studies/PineJS-Utility-Functions/#changesource)
