*   [Functions specific to the library](https://www.tradingview.com/charting-library-docs/v28/custom_studies/PineJS-Utility-Functions/#functions-specific-to-the-library)

---

# PineJS Utility Functions | Advanced Charts Documentation

[Skip to main content](https://www.tradingview.com/charting-library-docs/v26/custom_studies/PineJS-Utility-Functions/#__docusaurus_skipToContent_fallback)

This is documentation for **v26**, which is no longer actively maintained.

For up-to-date documentation, see the **[latest version](https://www.tradingview.com/charting-library-docs/)
**.

On this page

Custom Study [Constructor](https://www.tradingview.com/charting-library-docs/v26/custom_studies/Custom-Studies-Constructor)
 is able to use utility functions from PineJS object (an argument of [custom\_indicators\_getter](https://www.tradingview.com/charting-library-docs/v26/api/interfaces/Charting_Library.ChartingLibraryWidgetOptions#custom_indicators_getter)
 function).

Functions ported from Pine[​](https://www.tradingview.com/charting-library-docs/v26/custom_studies/PineJS-Utility-Functions/#functions-ported-from-pine "Direct link to Functions ported from Pine")

-----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

### `na(x)`[​](https://www.tradingview.com/charting-library-docs/v26/custom_studies/PineJS-Utility-Functions/#nax "Direct link to nax")

Test value if it's a NaN.

**Returns**

`true` if `x` is not a valid number (`x` is NaN), otherwise `false`.

### `nz(x, y)`[​](https://www.tradingview.com/charting-library-docs/v26/custom_studies/PineJS-Utility-Functions/#nzx-y "Direct link to nzx-y")

Replaces NaN values with zeros (or given value) in a series.

**Returns**

`x` if it's a valid (not NaN) number, otherwise y

### `and(expr1, expr2)`[​](https://www.tradingview.com/charting-library-docs/v26/custom_studies/PineJS-Utility-Functions/#andexpr1-expr2 "Direct link to andexpr1-expr2")

Logical AND. Applicable to boolean expressions.

**Returns**

Boolean value, or series of boolean values.

### `or(expr1, expr2)`[​](https://www.tradingview.com/charting-library-docs/v26/custom_studies/PineJS-Utility-Functions/#orexpr1-expr2 "Direct link to orexpr1-expr2")

Logical OR. Applicable to boolean expressions.

**Returns**

Boolean value, or series of boolean values.

### `not(expr)`[​](https://www.tradingview.com/charting-library-docs/v26/custom_studies/PineJS-Utility-Functions/#notexpr "Direct link to notexpr")

Logical negation (NOT). Applicable to boolean expressions.

**Returns**

Boolean value, or series of boolean values.

### `max(x1, x2, ..., x10)`[​](https://www.tradingview.com/charting-library-docs/v26/custom_studies/PineJS-Utility-Functions/#maxx1-x2--x10 "Direct link to maxx1-x2--x10")

**Returns**

The greatest of multiple values

### `min(x1, x2, ..., x10)`[​](https://www.tradingview.com/charting-library-docs/v26/custom_studies/PineJS-Utility-Functions/#minx1-x2--x10 "Direct link to minx1-x2--x10")

**Returns**

The smallest of multiple given values.

### `pow(base, exponent)`[​](https://www.tradingview.com/charting-library-docs/v26/custom_studies/PineJS-Utility-Functions/#powbase-exponent "Direct link to powbase-exponent")

Mathematical power function.

**Returns**

`x` raised to the power of `y`. If `x` is a series, it is calculated elementwise.

**Arguments**

*   **`base` (series\[float\])** Specify the base to use.
*   **`exponent` (float)** Specifies the exponent.

### `abs(x)`[​](https://www.tradingview.com/charting-library-docs/v26/custom_studies/PineJS-Utility-Functions/#absx "Direct link to absx")

Absolute value of `x` is `x` if `x >= 0`, or `-x` otherwise.

**Returns**

The absolute value of x

### `log(x)`[​](https://www.tradingview.com/charting-library-docs/v26/custom_studies/PineJS-Utility-Functions/#logx "Direct link to logx")

Natural logarithm of any `x > 0` is the unique `y` such that `e^y = x`

**Returns**

The natural logarithm of x.

### `log10(x)`[​](https://www.tradingview.com/charting-library-docs/v26/custom_studies/PineJS-Utility-Functions/#log10x "Direct link to log10x")

Base 10 logarithm of any `x > 0` is the unique `y` such that `10^y = x`

**Returns**

The base 10 logarithm of x.

### `sqrt(x)`[​](https://www.tradingview.com/charting-library-docs/v26/custom_studies/PineJS-Utility-Functions/#sqrtx "Direct link to sqrtx")

Square root of any `x >= 0` is the unique `y >= 0` such that `y^2 = x`

**Returns**

The square root of x.

### `sign(x)`[​](https://www.tradingview.com/charting-library-docs/v26/custom_studies/PineJS-Utility-Functions/#signx "Direct link to signx")

Sign (signum) of `x` is zero if the `x` is zero, `1.0` if the `x` is greater than zero, `-1.0` if the `x` is less than zero.

**Returns**

The sign of the argument.

### `exp(x)`[​](https://www.tradingview.com/charting-library-docs/v26/custom_studies/PineJS-Utility-Functions/#expx "Direct link to expx")

The exp function of `x` is `e^x`, where `x` is the argument and `e` is Euler's number.

**Returns**

A number representing e^x.

### `sin(x)`[​](https://www.tradingview.com/charting-library-docs/v26/custom_studies/PineJS-Utility-Functions/#sinx "Direct link to sinx")

The `sin` function returns the trigonometric sine of an angle.

**Returns**

The trigonometric sine of an angle.

**Arguments**

*   **`x` (series\[float\])** Angle, in radians.

### `cos(x)`[​](https://www.tradingview.com/charting-library-docs/v26/custom_studies/PineJS-Utility-Functions/#cosx "Direct link to cosx")

The cos function returns the trigonometric cosine of an angle.

**Returns**

The trigonometric cosine of an angle.

**Arguments**

*   **`x` (series\[float\])** Angle, in radians.

### `tan(x)`[​](https://www.tradingview.com/charting-library-docs/v26/custom_studies/PineJS-Utility-Functions/#tanx "Direct link to tanx")

The tan function returns the trigonometric tangent of an angle.

**Returns**

The trigonometric tangent of an angle.

**Arguments**

*   **`x` (series\[float\])** Angle, in radians.

### `asin(x)`[​](https://www.tradingview.com/charting-library-docs/v26/custom_studies/PineJS-Utility-Functions/#asinx "Direct link to asinx")

The asin function returns the arcsine (in radians) of number such that `sin(asin(y)) = y` for `y` in range `[-1, 1]`.

**Returns**

The arcsine of a value; the returned angle is in the range `[-Pi/2, Pi/2]`, or na if y is outside of range `[-1, 1]`.

### `acos(x)`[​](https://www.tradingview.com/charting-library-docs/v26/custom_studies/PineJS-Utility-Functions/#acosx "Direct link to acosx")

The acos function returns the arccosine (in radians) of number such that `cos(acos(y)) = y` for `y` in range `[-1, 1]`.

**Returns**

The arc cosine of a value; the returned angle is in the range `[0, Pi]`, or `na` if `y` is outside of range `[-1, 1]`.

### `atan(x)`[​](https://www.tradingview.com/charting-library-docs/v26/custom_studies/PineJS-Utility-Functions/#atanx "Direct link to atanx")

The atan function returns the arctangent (in radians) of number such that `tan(atan(y)) = y` for any `y`.

**Returns**

The arc tangent of a value; the returned angle is in the range `[-Pi/2, Pi/2]`.

### `floor(x)`[​](https://www.tradingview.com/charting-library-docs/v26/custom_studies/PineJS-Utility-Functions/#floorx "Direct link to floorx")

**Returns**

The largest integer less than or equal to the given number.

### `ceil(x)`[​](https://www.tradingview.com/charting-library-docs/v26/custom_studies/PineJS-Utility-Functions/#ceilx "Direct link to ceilx")

The ceil function returns the smallest (closest to negative infinity) integer that is greater than or equal to the argument.

**Returns**

The smallest integer greater than or equal to the given number.

### `round(x)`[​](https://www.tradingview.com/charting-library-docs/v26/custom_studies/PineJS-Utility-Functions/#roundx "Direct link to roundx")

**Returns**

The value of `x` rounded to the nearest integer, with ties rounding up. If the precision parameter is used, returns a float value rounded to that number of decimal places.

### `avg(x1, x2, ..., x10)`[​](https://www.tradingview.com/charting-library-docs/v26/custom_studies/PineJS-Utility-Functions/#avgx1-x2--x10 "Direct link to avgx1-x2--x10")

Calculates average of all given series (elementwise).

**Returns**

Average.

### `open(context)`[​](https://www.tradingview.com/charting-library-docs/v26/custom_studies/PineJS-Utility-Functions/#opencontext "Direct link to opencontext")

Current open price.

**Arguments**

*   **`context`** PineJS execution context.

### `high(context)`[​](https://www.tradingview.com/charting-library-docs/v26/custom_studies/PineJS-Utility-Functions/#highcontext "Direct link to highcontext")

Current high price.

**Arguments**

*   **`context`** PineJS execution context.

### `low(context)`[​](https://www.tradingview.com/charting-library-docs/v26/custom_studies/PineJS-Utility-Functions/#lowcontext "Direct link to lowcontext")

Current low price.

**Arguments**

*   **`context`** PineJS execution context.

### `close(context)`[​](https://www.tradingview.com/charting-library-docs/v26/custom_studies/PineJS-Utility-Functions/#closecontext "Direct link to closecontext")

Close price of the current bar when it has closed, or last traded price of a yet incomplete, realtime bar.

**Arguments**

*   **`context`** PineJS execution context.

### `hl2(context)`[​](https://www.tradingview.com/charting-library-docs/v26/custom_studies/PineJS-Utility-Functions/#hl2context "Direct link to hl2context")

Is a shortcut for `(high + low)/2`

**Arguments**

*   **`context`** PineJS execution context.

### `hlc3(context)`[​](https://www.tradingview.com/charting-library-docs/v26/custom_studies/PineJS-Utility-Functions/#hlc3context "Direct link to hlc3context")

Is a shortcut for (high + low + close)/3

**Arguments**

*   **`context`** PineJS execution context.

### `ohlc4(context)`[​](https://www.tradingview.com/charting-library-docs/v26/custom_studies/PineJS-Utility-Functions/#ohlc4context "Direct link to ohlc4context")

Is a shortcut for (open + high + low + close)/4

**Arguments**

*   **`context`** PineJS execution context.

### `volume(context)`[​](https://www.tradingview.com/charting-library-docs/v26/custom_studies/PineJS-Utility-Functions/#volumecontext "Direct link to volumecontext")

Current bar volume.

**Arguments**

*   **`context`** PineJS execution context.

### `period(context)`[​](https://www.tradingview.com/charting-library-docs/v26/custom_studies/PineJS-Utility-Functions/#periodcontext "Direct link to periodcontext")

Resolution string, e.g. `60` - 60 minutes, `D` - daily, `W` - weekly, `M` - monthly, `5D` - 5 days, `12M` - one year, `3M` - one quarter

**Arguments**

*   **`context`** PineJS execution context.

### `tickerid(context)`[​](https://www.tradingview.com/charting-library-docs/v26/custom_studies/PineJS-Utility-Functions/#tickeridcontext "Direct link to tickeridcontext")

**Arguments**

*   **`context`** PineJS execution context.

### `year(context)`[​](https://www.tradingview.com/charting-library-docs/v26/custom_studies/PineJS-Utility-Functions/#yearcontext "Direct link to yearcontext")

Current bar year in exchange timezone.

**Arguments**

*   **`context`** PineJS execution context.

### `month(context)`[​](https://www.tradingview.com/charting-library-docs/v26/custom_studies/PineJS-Utility-Functions/#monthcontext "Direct link to monthcontext")

Current bar month in exchange timezone.

**Arguments**

*   **`context`** PineJS execution context.

### `weekofyear(context)`[​](https://www.tradingview.com/charting-library-docs/v26/custom_studies/PineJS-Utility-Functions/#weekofyearcontext "Direct link to weekofyearcontext")

Week number of current bar time in exchange timezone.

**Arguments**

*   **`context`** PineJS execution context.

### `dayofmonth(context)`[​](https://www.tradingview.com/charting-library-docs/v26/custom_studies/PineJS-Utility-Functions/#dayofmonthcontext "Direct link to dayofmonthcontext")

Date of current bar time in exchange timezone.

**Arguments**

*   **`context`** PineJS execution context.

### `dayofweek(context)`[​](https://www.tradingview.com/charting-library-docs/v26/custom_studies/PineJS-Utility-Functions/#dayofweekcontext "Direct link to dayofweekcontext")

Day of week for current bar time in exchange timezone.

**Arguments**

*   **`context`** PineJS execution context.

### `hour(context)`[​](https://www.tradingview.com/charting-library-docs/v26/custom_studies/PineJS-Utility-Functions/#hourcontext "Direct link to hourcontext")

Current bar hour in exchange timezone.

**Arguments**

*   **`context`** PineJS execution context.

### `minute(context)`[​](https://www.tradingview.com/charting-library-docs/v26/custom_studies/PineJS-Utility-Functions/#minutecontext "Direct link to minutecontext")

Current bar minute in exchange timezone.

**Arguments**

*   **`context`** PineJS execution context.

### `second(context)`[​](https://www.tradingview.com/charting-library-docs/v26/custom_studies/PineJS-Utility-Functions/#secondcontext "Direct link to secondcontext")

Current bar second in exchange timezone.

**Arguments**

*   **`context`** PineJS execution context.

### `iff(condition, then, _else)`[​](https://www.tradingview.com/charting-library-docs/v26/custom_studies/PineJS-Utility-Functions/#iffcondition-then-_else "Direct link to iffcondition-then-_else")

If ... then ... else ...

**Remarks**

`iff` does exactly the same thing as ternary conditional operator `?:` but in a functional style. Also iff is slightly less efficient than operator `?:`

### `rising(series, length)`[​](https://www.tradingview.com/charting-library-docs/v26/custom_studies/PineJS-Utility-Functions/#risingseries-length "Direct link to risingseries-length")

Test if the `series` is now rising for `length` bars long.

**Returns**

`true` if current `x` is greater than any previous `x` for `length` bars back, `false` otherwise.

**Arguments**

*   **`series` (series\[float\])** Series of values to process.
*   **`length` (integer)** Number of bars (length).

### `falling(series, length)`[​](https://www.tradingview.com/charting-library-docs/v26/custom_studies/PineJS-Utility-Functions/#fallingseries-length "Direct link to fallingseries-length")

Test if the `series` is now falling for `length` bars long.

**Returns**

`true` if current `x` is less than any previous `x` for `length` bars back, `false` otherwise.

**Arguments**

*   **`series` (series\[float\])** Series of values to process.
*   **`length` (integer)** Number of bars (length).

### `rsi(upper, lower)`[​](https://www.tradingview.com/charting-library-docs/v26/custom_studies/PineJS-Utility-Functions/#rsiupper-lower "Direct link to rsiupper-lower")

Relative strength index. It is calculated based on rma's of upward and downward change of x.

**Returns**

Relative strength index.

**Arguments**

*   **`upper` (float)** Series of values to process.
*   **`lower` (float)** Number of bars (length).

### `sum(source, length, context)`[​](https://www.tradingview.com/charting-library-docs/v26/custom_studies/PineJS-Utility-Functions/#sumsource-length-context "Direct link to sumsource-length-context")

The sum function returns the sliding sum of last y values of x.

**Returns**

Sum of x for y bars back.

**Arguments**

*   **`source` (series\[float\])** Series of values to process.
*   **`length` (integer)** Number of bars (length).
*   **`context`** PineJS execution context.

### `sma(source, length, context)`[​](https://www.tradingview.com/charting-library-docs/v26/custom_studies/PineJS-Utility-Functions/#smasource-length-context "Direct link to smasource-length-context")

The sma function returns the moving average, that is the sum of last `length` values of `source`, divided by `length`.

**Returns**

Simple moving average of x for y bars back.

**Arguments**

*   **`source` (series\[float\])** Series of values to process.
*   **`length` (integer)** Number of bars (length).
*   **`context`** PineJS execution context.

### `rma(source, length, context)`[​](https://www.tradingview.com/charting-library-docs/v26/custom_studies/PineJS-Utility-Functions/#rmasource-length-context "Direct link to rmasource-length-context")

Moving average used in RSI. It is the exponentially weighted moving average with alpha = 1 / length.

**Returns**

Exponential moving average of x with alpha = 1 / y.

**Arguments**

*   **`source` (series\[float\])** Series of values to process.
*   **`length` (integer)** Number of bars (length).
*   **`context`** PineJS execution context.

### `fixnan(current, context)`[​](https://www.tradingview.com/charting-library-docs/v26/custom_studies/PineJS-Utility-Functions/#fixnancurrent-context "Direct link to fixnancurrent-context")

For a given series replaces NaN values with previous nearest non-NaN value.

**Returns**

Series without `na` gaps.

**Arguments**

*   **`current` (series\[float\])** Series of values to process.
*   **`context`** PineJS execution context.

### `tr(handle_nan, context)`[​](https://www.tradingview.com/charting-library-docs/v26/custom_studies/PineJS-Utility-Functions/#trhandle_nan-context "Direct link to trhandle_nan-context")

**Returns**

True range. It is `max(high - low, abs(high - close[1]), abs(low - close[1]))`

**Arguments**

*   **`handle_nan` (bool)** How NaN values are handled. if `true`, and previous day's close is NaN then `tr` would be calculated as current day high-low. Otherwise (if `false`) `tr` would return NaN in such cases. Also note, that atr uses `tr(true)`.
*   **`context`** PineJS execution context.

### `atr(length, context)`[​](https://www.tradingview.com/charting-library-docs/v26/custom_studies/PineJS-Utility-Functions/#atrlength-context "Direct link to atrlength-context")

Function atr (average true range) returns the RMA of true range. True range is `max(high - low, abs(high - close[1]), abs(low - close[1]))`

**Returns**

Average true range.

**Arguments**

*   **`length` (integer)** Length (number of bars back).
*   **`context`** PineJS execution context.

### `ema(series, length, context)`[​](https://www.tradingview.com/charting-library-docs/v26/custom_studies/PineJS-Utility-Functions/#emaseries-length-context "Direct link to emaseries-length-context")

The ema function returns the exponentially weighted moving average. In ema weighting factors decrease exponentially. It calculates by using a formula: `EMA = alpha * x + (1 - alpha) * EMA[1]`, where `alpha = 2 / (y + 1)`

**Returns**

Exponential moving average of x with alpha = 2 / (y + 1)

**Arguments**

*   **`source` (series\[float\])** Series of values to process.
*   **`length` (integer)** Number of bars (length).
*   **`context`** PineJS execution context.

### `wma(source, length, context)`[​](https://www.tradingview.com/charting-library-docs/v26/custom_studies/PineJS-Utility-Functions/#wmasource-length-context "Direct link to wmasource-length-context")

The wma function returns weighted moving average of `source` for `length` bars back. In wma weighting factors decrease in arithmetical progression.

**Returns**

Weighted moving average of `series` for `length` bars back.

**Arguments**

*   **`source` (series\[float\])** Series of values to process.
*   **`length` (integer)** Number of bars (length).
*   **`context`** PineJS execution context.

### `vwma(source, length, context)`[​](https://www.tradingview.com/charting-library-docs/v26/custom_studies/PineJS-Utility-Functions/#vwmasource-length-context "Direct link to vwmasource-length-context")

The `vwma` function returns volume-weighted moving average of `source` for `length` bars back. It is the same as: `sma(x * volume, y) / sma(volume, y)`

**Returns**

Volume-weighted moving average of `source` for `length` bars back.

**Arguments**

*   **`source` (series\[float\])** Series of values to process.
*   **`length` (integer)** Number of bars (length).
*   **`context`** PineJS execution context.

### `swma(source, context)`[​](https://www.tradingview.com/charting-library-docs/v26/custom_studies/PineJS-Utility-Functions/#swmasource-context "Direct link to swmasource-context")

Symmetrically weighted moving average with fixed length: 4. Weights: `[1/6, 2/6, 2/6, 1/6]`.

**Returns**

Symmetrically weighted moving average

**Arguments**

*   **`source` (series\[float\])** Source series.
*   **`context`** PineJS execution context.

### `lowestbars(source, length, context)`[​](https://www.tradingview.com/charting-library-docs/v26/custom_studies/PineJS-Utility-Functions/#lowestbarssource-length-context "Direct link to lowestbarssource-length-context")

Lowest value offset for a given number of bars back.

**Returns**

Offset to the lowest bar.

**Arguments**

*   **`source` (series\[float\])** Series of values to process.
*   **`length` (integer)** Number of bars (length).
*   **`context`** PineJS execution context.

### `lowest(source, length, context)`[​](https://www.tradingview.com/charting-library-docs/v26/custom_studies/PineJS-Utility-Functions/#lowestsource-length-context "Direct link to lowestsource-length-context")

Lowest value for a given number of bars back.

**Returns**

Lowest value.

**Arguments**

*   **`source` (series\[float\])** Series of values to process.
*   **`length` (integer)** Number of bars (length).
*   **`context`** PineJS execution context.

### `highestbars(source, length, context)`[​](https://www.tradingview.com/charting-library-docs/v26/custom_studies/PineJS-Utility-Functions/#highestbarssource-length-context "Direct link to highestbarssource-length-context")

Highest value offset for a given number of bars back.

**Returns**

Offset to the highest bar.

**Arguments**

*   **`source` (series\[float\])** Series of values to process.
*   **`length` (integer)** Number of bars (length).
*   **`context`** PineJS execution context.

### `highest(source, length, context)`[​](https://www.tradingview.com/charting-library-docs/v26/custom_studies/PineJS-Utility-Functions/#highestsource-length-context "Direct link to highestsource-length-context")

Highest value for a given number of bars back.

**Returns**

Highest value.

**Arguments**

*   **`source` (series\[float\])** Series of values to process.
*   **`length` (integer)** Number of bars (length).
*   **`context`** PineJS execution context.

### `cum(x, context)`[​](https://www.tradingview.com/charting-library-docs/v26/custom_studies/PineJS-Utility-Functions/#cumx-context "Direct link to cumx-context")

Cumulative (total) sum of `x`. In other words it's a sum of all elements of `x`.

**Returns**

Total sum series.

**Arguments**

*   **`x` (series\[float\])** Series of values to process.
*   **`context`** PineJS execution context.

### `accdist(context)`[​](https://www.tradingview.com/charting-library-docs/v26/custom_studies/PineJS-Utility-Functions/#accdistcontext "Direct link to accdistcontext")

Accumulation/distribution index.

**Arguments**

*   **`context`** PineJS execution context.

### `correlation(source_a, source_b, length, context)`[​](https://www.tradingview.com/charting-library-docs/v26/custom_studies/PineJS-Utility-Functions/#correlationsource_a-source_b-length-context "Direct link to correlationsource_a-source_b-length-context")

Correlation coefficient. Describes the degree to which two series tend to deviate from their `sma` values.

**Returns**

Correlation coefficient.

**Arguments**

*   **`source_a` (series\[float\])** Source series.
*   **`source_b` (series\[float\])** Target series.
*   **`length` (integer)** Length (number of bars back).
*   **`context`** PineJS execution context.

### `stoch(source, high, low, length, context)`[​](https://www.tradingview.com/charting-library-docs/v26/custom_studies/PineJS-Utility-Functions/#stochsource-high-low-length-context "Direct link to stochsource-high-low-length-context")

Stochastic. It is calculated by a formula: `100 * (close - lowest(low, length)) / (highest(high, length) - lowest(low, length))`

**Returns**

Stochastic.

**Arguments**

*   **`source` (series\[float\])** Source series.
*   **`high` (series\[float\])** Series of high.
*   **`low` (series\[float\])** Series of low.
*   **`length` (integer)** Length (number of bars back).
*   **`context`** PineJS execution context.

### `tsi(source, short_length, long_length, context)`[​](https://www.tradingview.com/charting-library-docs/v26/custom_studies/PineJS-Utility-Functions/#tsisource-short_length-long_length-context "Direct link to tsisource-short_length-long_length-context")

True strength index. It uses moving averages of the underlying momentum of a financial instrument.

**Returns**

True strength index. A value in range \[-1, 1\]

**Arguments**

*   **`source` (series\[float\])** Source series.
*   **`short_length` (integer)** Short length.
*   **`long_length` (integer)** Long length.
*   **`context`** PineJS execution context.

### `cross(x, y, context)`[​](https://www.tradingview.com/charting-library-docs/v26/custom_studies/PineJS-Utility-Functions/#crossx-y-context "Direct link to crossx-y-context")

**Returns**

`true` if two series have crossed each other, otherwise `false`.

**Arguments**

*   **`x` (series\[float\])** First series.
*   **`y` (series\[float\])** Second series.
*   **`context`** PineJS execution context.

### `linreg(source, length, offset)`[​](https://www.tradingview.com/charting-library-docs/v26/custom_studies/PineJS-Utility-Functions/#linregsource-length-offset "Direct link to linregsource-length-offset")

Linear regression curve. A line that best fits the prices specified over a user-defined time period. It is calculated using the least squares method. The result of this function is calculated using the formula: `linreg = intercept + slope * (length - 1 - offset)`, where intercept and slope are the values calculated with the least squares method on source series (x argument).

**Returns**

Linear regression curve.

**Arguments**

*   **`source` (series\[float\])** Source series.
*   **`length` (integer)**
*   **`offset` (integer)** Offset.

### `sar(start, inc, max, context)`[​](https://www.tradingview.com/charting-library-docs/v26/custom_studies/PineJS-Utility-Functions/#sarstart-inc-max-context "Direct link to sarstart-inc-max-context")

Parabolic SAR (parabolic stop and reverse) is a method devised by J. Welles Wilder, Jr., to find potential reversals in the market price direction of traded goods.

**Returns**

Parabolic SAR.

**Arguments**

*   **`start` (series\[float\])** Start.
*   **`inc` (integer)** Increment.
*   **`max` (integer)** Maximum.
*   **`context`** PineJS execution context.

### `alma(series, length, offset, sigma)`[​](https://www.tradingview.com/charting-library-docs/v26/custom_studies/PineJS-Utility-Functions/#almaseries-length-offset-sigma "Direct link to almaseries-length-offset-sigma")

Arnaud Legoux Moving Average. It uses Gaussian distribution as weights for moving average.

**Returns**

Arnaud Legoux Moving Average.

**Arguments**

*   **`series` (series\[float\])** Series of values to process.
*   **`length` (integer)** Number of bars (length).
*   **`offset` (float)** Controls tradeoff between smoothness (closer to 1) and responsiveness (closer to 0).
*   **`sigma` (float)** Changes the smoothness of ALMA. The larger sigma the smoother ALMA.

### `change(source)`[​](https://www.tradingview.com/charting-library-docs/v26/custom_studies/PineJS-Utility-Functions/#changesource "Direct link to changesource")

Difference between current value and previous, `x - x[1]`.

**Returns**

The result of subtraction.

**Arguments**

*   **`source` (series\[float\])**

### `roc(source, length)`[​](https://www.tradingview.com/charting-library-docs/v26/custom_studies/PineJS-Utility-Functions/#rocsource-length "Direct link to rocsource-length")

Function roc (rate of change) showing the difference between current value of `source` and the value of `source` that was `length` days ago. It is calculated by the formula: `100 * change(src, length) / src[length]`.

**Returns**

The rate of change of `source` for `length` bars back.

**Arguments**

*   **`source` (series\[float\])** Series of values to process.
*   **`length` (integer)** Number of bars (length).

### `dev(source, length, context)`[​](https://www.tradingview.com/charting-library-docs/v26/custom_studies/PineJS-Utility-Functions/#devsource-length-context "Direct link to devsource-length-context")

Measure of difference between the series and it's `sma`.

**Returns**

Deviation of `source` for `length` bars back.

**Arguments**

*   **`source` (series\[float\])** Series of values to process.
*   **`length` (integer)** Number of bars (length).
*   **`context`** PineJS execution context.

### `stdev(source, length, context)`[​](https://www.tradingview.com/charting-library-docs/v26/custom_studies/PineJS-Utility-Functions/#stdevsource-length-context "Direct link to stdevsource-length-context")

**Returns**

Standard deviation.

**Arguments**

*   **`source` (series\[float\])** Series of values to process.
*   **`length` (integer)** Number of bars (length).
*   **`context`** PineJS execution context.

**Remarks**

This is a biased estimation of standard deviation.

### `variance(source, length, context)`[​](https://www.tradingview.com/charting-library-docs/v26/custom_studies/PineJS-Utility-Functions/#variancesource-length-context "Direct link to variancesource-length-context")

Variance is the expectation of the squared deviation of a series from its mean `sma`, and it informally measures how far a set of numbers are spread out from their mean.

**Returns**

Variance of `source` for `length` bars back.

**Arguments**

*   **`source` (series\[float\])** Series of values to process.
*   **`length` (integer)** Number of bars (length).
*   **`context`** PineJS execution context.

**Remarks**

This is a biased estimation of sample variance.

### `percentrank(source, length)`[​](https://www.tradingview.com/charting-library-docs/v26/custom_studies/PineJS-Utility-Functions/#percentranksource-length "Direct link to percentranksource-length")

Percent rank is the percents of how many previous values was less than or equal to the current value of given series.

**Returns**

Percent rank of `source` for `length` bars back.

**Arguments**

*   **`source` (series\[float\])** Series of values to process.
*   **`length` (integer)** Number of bars (length).

### `dmi(diLength, adxSmoothingLength, context)`[​](https://www.tradingview.com/charting-library-docs/v26/custom_studies/PineJS-Utility-Functions/#dmidilength-adxsmoothinglength-context "Direct link to dmidilength-adxsmoothinglength-context")

Calculates the directional movement values +DI, -DI, DX, ADX, and ADXR.

**Returns**

An array of the +DI, -DI, DX, ADX, and ADXR values with `diLength` smoothing for the (+/-)DI values and `adxSmoothingLength` for the ADX value.

**Arguments**

*   **`diLength` (integer)** Number of bars (length) used when calculating the +DI and -DI values.
*   **`adxSmoothingLength` (integer)** Number of bars (length) used when calculating the ADX value.
*   **`context`** PineJS execution context.

Functions specific to the library[​](https://www.tradingview.com/charting-library-docs/v26/custom_studies/PineJS-Utility-Functions/#functions-specific-to-the-library "Direct link to Functions specific to the library")

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

*   `isZero(number)` - boolean, shows whether the `number` equals zero or almost zero
*   `toBool(number)` - boolean, `true` if a number is finite and not equals zero
*   `eq(arg1, arg2)` - boolean, `true` if argument numbers are equal
*   `neq(arg1, arg2)` - boolean, `true` if argument numbers are not equal
*   `ge(arg1, arg2)` - boolean, `true` if first argument number is greater or equal to second
*   `gt(arg1, arg2)` - boolean, `true` if first argument number is greater than second
*   `le(arg1, arg2)` - boolean, `true` if first argument number is lesser or equal to second
*   `lt(arg1, arg2)` - boolean, `true` if first argument is lesser than second
*   `updatetime(context)` - string, symbol update time
*   `ticker(context)` - string, symbol name without exchange prefix, e.g. 'MSFT'
*   `interval(context)` - returns symbol interval string
*   `isdwm(context)` - boolean, returns `true` if current resolution is a daily or weekly or monthly resolution
*   `isintraday(context)` - boolean, returns true if current resolution is an intraday (minutes or seconds) resolution
*   `isdaily(context)` - boolean, returns `true` if current resolution is a daily resolution
*   `isweekly(context)` - boolean, returns `true` if current resolution is a weekly resolution
*   `ismonthly(context)` - boolean, returns `true` if current resolution is a monthly resolution
*   `add_days_considering_dst(timezone, utcTime, daysCount)`
*   `selectSessionBreaks(context, times)` - select session breaks for intraday resolutions only
*   `timepart(symbol, field, time)`
*   `wvap(source, context)`
*   `createNewSessionCheck(context)`
*   `error(message)` - returns study error with `message` specified
*   `time(context, period, spec)` - returns UNIX time of current bar

*   [Functions ported from Pine](https://www.tradingview.com/charting-library-docs/v26/custom_studies/PineJS-Utility-Functions/#functions-ported-from-pine)
    *   [`na(x)`](https://www.tradingview.com/charting-library-docs/v26/custom_studies/PineJS-Utility-Functions/#nax)

    *   [`highest(source, length, context)`](https://www.tradingview.com/charting-library-docs/v26/custom_studies/PineJS-Utility-Functions/#highestsource-length-context)
        
    *   [`cum(x, context)`](https://www.tradingview.com/charting-library-docs/v26/custom_studies/PineJS-Utility-Functions/#cumx-context)
        
    *   [`accdist(context)`](https://www.tradingview.com/charting-library-docs/v26/custom_studies/PineJS-Utility-Functions/#accdistcontext)
        
    *   [`correlation(source_a, source_b, length, context)`](https://www.tradingview.com/charting-library-docs/v26/custom_studies/PineJS-Utility-Functions/#correlationsource_a-source_b-length-context)
        
    *   [`stoch(source, high, low, length, context)`](https://www.tradingview.com/charting-library-docs/v26/custom_studies/PineJS-Utility-Functions/#stochsource-high-low-length-context)
        
    *   [`tsi(source, short_length, long_length, context)`](https://www.tradingview.com/charting-library-docs/v26/custom_studies/PineJS-Utility-Functions/#tsisource-short_length-long_length-context)
        
    *   [`cross(x, y, context)`](https://www.tradingview.com/charting-library-docs/v26/custom_studies/PineJS-Utility-Functions/#crossx-y-context)
        
    *   [`linreg(source, length, offset)`](https://www.tradingview.com/charting-library-docs/v26/custom_studies/PineJS-Utility-Functions/#linregsource-length-offset)
        
    *   [`sar(start, inc, max, context)`](https://www.tradingview.com/charting-library-docs/v26/custom_studies/PineJS-Utility-Functions/#sarstart-inc-max-context)
        
    *   [`alma(series, length, offset, sigma)`](https://www.tradingview.com/charting-library-docs/v26/custom_studies/PineJS-Utility-Functions/#almaseries-length-offset-sigma)
        
    *   [`change(source)`](https://www.tradingview.com/charting-library-docs/v26/custom_studies/PineJS-Utility-Functions/#changesource)
        
    *   [`roc(source, length)`](https://www.tradingview.com/charting-library-docs/v26/custom_studies/PineJS-Utility-Functions/#rocsource-length)
        
    *   [`dev(source, length, context)`](https://www.tradingview.com/charting-library-docs/v26/custom_studies/PineJS-Utility-Functions/#devsource-length-context)
        
    *   [`stdev(source, length, context)`](https://www.tradingview.com/charting-library-docs/v26/custom_studies/PineJS-Utility-Functions/#stdevsource-length-context)
        
    *   [`variance(source, length, context)`](https://www.tradingview.com/charting-library-docs/v26/custom_studies/PineJS-Utility-Functions/#variancesource-length-context)
        
    *   [`percentrank(source, length)`](https://www.tradingview.com/charting-library-docs/v26/custom_studies/PineJS-Utility-Functions/#percentranksource-length)
        
    *   [`dmi(diLength, adxSmoothingLength, context)`](https://www.tradingview.com/charting-library-docs/v26/custom_studies/PineJS-Utility-Functions/#dmidilength-adxsmoothinglength-context)
        
*   [Functions specific to the library](https://www.tradingview.com/charting-library-docs/v26/custom_studies/PineJS-Utility-Functions/#functions-specific-to-the-library)

# Symbol Status | Advanced Charts Documentation

[Skip to main content](https://www.tradingview.com/charting-library-docs/v26/ui_elements/Symbol-Status/#__docusaurus_skipToContent_fallback)

This is documentation for **v26**, which is no longer actively maintained.

For up-to-date documentation, see the **[latest version](https://www.tradingview.com/charting-library-docs/)
**.

On this page

Market Status[​](https://www.tradingview.com/charting-library-docs/v26/ui_elements/Symbol-Status/#market-status "Direct link to Market Status")

------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

By default the library will display the current market status as an icon within the legend for the main series of the chart.

![Market Status Example](https://www.tradingview.com/charting-library-docs/v26/assets/images/market-status-example-ef63a72532b30d3e20db32e92ba7e380.png)

You can hide the market status by adding the [display\_market\_status](https://www.tradingview.com/charting-library-docs/v26/customization/Featuresets)
 featureset to the [disabled\_features](https://www.tradingview.com/charting-library-docs/v26/api/interfaces/Charting_Library.ChartingLibraryWidgetOptions#disabled_features)
. Additionally, the market status visibility can be configured by the user within the 'Status line' section of the chart settings dialog.

Additional Custom Symbol Status[​](https://www.tradingview.com/charting-library-docs/v26/ui_elements/Symbol-Status/#additional-custom-symbol-status "Direct link to Additional Custom Symbol Status")

------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

It is possible to provide an additional custom status for specific symbols by using the [ICustomSymbolStatusApi](https://www.tradingview.com/charting-library-docs/v26/api/interfaces/Charting_Library.ICustomSymbolStatusApi)
 API. The API can be accessed via the [customSymbolStatus](https://www.tradingview.com/charting-library-docs/v26/api/interfaces/Charting_Library.IChartingLibraryWidget#customsymbolstatus)
 method on the widget.

warning

It is important that the symbol id provided to the API exactly matches the resolved id. You can get the id for a current symbol of a chart by using the [symbol](https://www.tradingview.com/charting-library-docs/v26/api/interfaces/Charting_Library.IChartWidgetApi#symbol)
 method.

The status assigned to a symbol will be visible on all charts displaying that symbol.

tip

The custom status for a symbol can be set at any time, whether the symbol has been loaded, displayed, or neither.

### Example[​](https://www.tradingview.com/charting-library-docs/v26/ui_elements/Symbol-Status/#example "Direct link to Example")

![Custom Status Example](https://www.tradingview.com/charting-library-docs/v26/assets/images/custom-status-example-3fd07217ff275acd89ff43d428744646.png)

    const myCustomIconSvgString = `<svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" viewBox="0 0 20 20" fill="currentColor"><path fill-rule="evenodd" d="M13.5 4.938a7 7 0 11-9.006 1.737c.202-.257.59-.218.793.039.278.352.594.672.943.954.332.269.786-.049.773-.476a5.977 5.977 0 01.572-2.759 6.026 6.026 0 012.486-2.665c.247-.14.55-.016.677.238A6.967 6.967 0 0013.5 4.938zM14 12a4 4 0 01-4 4c-1.913 0-3.52-1.398-3.91-3.182-.093-.429.44-.643.814-.413a4.043 4.043 0 001.601.564c.303.038.531-.24.51-.544a5.975 5.975 0 011.315-4.192.447.447 0 01.431-.16A4.001 4.001 0 0114 12z" clip-rule="evenodd" /></svg>`;// icon source: https://heroicons.comwidget  .customSymbolStatus()  .symbol('NASDAQNM:AAPL') // select the symbol  .setVisible(true) // make the status visible  .setColor('rgb(255, 40, 60)') // set the colour  .setIcon(myCustomIconSvgString) // string for an svg icon, i.e. '<svg> ... </svg>'  .setTooltip('Tooltip') // text to be displayed within the hover tooltip  .setDropDownContent([    // content to be displayed within the large pop-up tooltip    {      title: 'Title', // title to be displayed within the pop-up      color: 'rgb(255, 60, 70)', // optional, if you want it to be different to above      content: ['Explanation of status', '<br/><br/>', 'More details...'],      action: {        // Optional action to be displayed        text: 'Read more here',        tooltip: 'opens in a new window',        onClick: () => {          window.open('https://www.tradingview.com/', '_blank');        },      },    },  ]);

*   [Market Status](https://www.tradingview.com/charting-library-docs/v26/ui_elements/Symbol-Status/#market-status)
    
*   [Additional Custom Symbol Status](https://www.tradingview.com/charting-library-docs/v26/ui_elements/Symbol-Status/#additional-custom-symbol-status)
    *   [Example](https://www.tradingview.com/charting-library-docs/v26/ui_elements/Symbol-Status/#example)

---

# Time scale | Advanced Charts Documentation

[Skip to main content](https://www.tradingview.com/charting-library-docs/v26/ui_elements/Time-Scale/#__docusaurus_skipToContent_fallback)

This is documentation for **v26**, which is no longer actively maintained.

For up-to-date documentation, see the **[latest version](https://www.tradingview.com/charting-library-docs/)
**.

On this page

**Time scale** (or time axis) is a horizontal scale at the bottom of the chart that displays the time of bars.

![Time scale](https://www.tradingview.com/charting-library-docs/v26/assets/images/time-scale-3196a6556602023a8b3700cf1d833407.png)

Time scale API[​](https://www.tradingview.com/charting-library-docs/v26/ui_elements/Time-Scale/#time-scale-api "Direct link to Time scale API")

------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

You can manage the time scale using the Time scale API. For example, you can set a specific right margin or detect when the chart has been zoomed in/out. Refer to [`ITimeScaleApi`](https://www.tradingview.com/charting-library-docs/v26/api/interfaces/Charting_Library.ITimeScaleApi)
 for more information.

Date and time formatting[​](https://www.tradingview.com/charting-library-docs/v26/ui_elements/Time-Scale/#date-and-time-formatting "Direct link to Date and time formatting")

------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

You can adjust the display format of date and time values using the [`custom_formatters`](https://www.tradingview.com/charting-library-docs/v26/api/interfaces/Charting_Library.ChartingLibraryWidgetOptions#custom_formatters)
 property of the [Widget Constructor](https://www.tradingview.com/charting-library-docs/v26/core_concepts/Widget-Constructor)
. Check out the [Widget Constructor tutorial](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=bdvmM3FNnSY&t=1466s)
 on YouTube for an implementation example.

Time frame toolbar[​](https://www.tradingview.com/charting-library-docs/v26/ui_elements/Time-Scale/#time-frame-toolbar "Direct link to Time frame toolbar")

------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Time frames are time period buttons displayed at the bottom-left corner of the chart. When users switch a time frame, it causes the following changes to satisfy the selected time frame:

1.  The chart [resolution](https://www.tradingview.com/charting-library-docs/v26/core_concepts/Resolution)
     changes.
2.  The bars scale horizontally to cover the entire requested date/time range.

For example, clicking `1Y` makes the chart switch the resolution to `1W` and scale it accordingly to display weekly bars for the entire year. Each time frame has its default chart resolution:

| Time frame | Chart resolution |
| --- | --- |
| 5Y  | W   |
| 1Y  | W   |
| 6M  | 120 |
| 3M  | 60  |
| 1M  | 30  |
| 5D  | 5   |
| 1D  | 1   |

### Time frame customization[​](https://www.tradingview.com/charting-library-docs/v26/ui_elements/Time-Scale/#time-frame-customization "Direct link to Time frame customization")

You can customize displayed time frames using the [`time_frames`](https://www.tradingview.com/charting-library-docs/v26/api/interfaces/Charting_Library.ChartingLibraryWidgetOptions#time_frames)
 property of the [Widget Constructor](https://www.tradingview.com/charting-library-docs/v26/core_concepts/Widget-Constructor)
. Note that time frames that require resolutions that are unavailable for a particular symbol will be hidden. You can also check out the [Widget Constructor tutorial](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=bdvmM3FNnSY&t=2328s)
 on YouTube for an implementation example.

### Programmatic time frame setting[​](https://www.tradingview.com/charting-library-docs/v26/ui_elements/Time-Scale/#programmatic-time-frame-setting "Direct link to Programmatic time frame setting")

If you want to programmatically set the time frame for the active chart, use the [`setTimeFrame`](https://www.tradingview.com/charting-library-docs/v26/api/interfaces/Charting_Library.IChartWidgetApi#settimeframe)
 method of the [`IChartWidgetApi`](https://www.tradingview.com/charting-library-docs/v26/api/interfaces/Charting_Library.IChartWidgetApi)
. For example, the following code snippet applies the `1Y` time frame, which is the same as when users click `1Y` at the bottom of the chart:

    const widget = new TradingView.widget(/* Widget properties */);widget.onChartReady(() => {    const chart = widget.chart();    chart.setTimeFrame({val: {type: 'period-back', value: '12M'}, res: '1W'});});

Extended time scale[​](https://www.tradingview.com/charting-library-docs/v26/ui_elements/Time-Scale/#extended-time-scale "Direct link to Extended time scale")

---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

You can enable indicators to extend the time scale. This allows you to create custom indicators that show bars at a higher resolution than the main series. Refer to [Extending the time scale](https://www.tradingview.com/charting-library-docs/v26/custom_studies/Studies-Extending-The-Time-Scale)
 for more information.

Extended sessions[​](https://www.tradingview.com/charting-library-docs/v26/ui_elements/Time-Scale/#extended-sessions "Direct link to Extended sessions")

---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

If you want to display extended sessions for some symbols, refer to the [Extended sessions](https://www.tradingview.com/charting-library-docs/v26/connecting_data/Extended-Sessions)
 article for more information.

*   [Time scale API](https://www.tradingview.com/charting-library-docs/v26/ui_elements/Time-Scale/#time-scale-api)
    
*   [Date and time formatting](https://www.tradingview.com/charting-library-docs/v26/ui_elements/Time-Scale/#date-and-time-formatting)
    
*   [Time frame toolbar](https://www.tradingview.com/charting-library-docs/v26/ui_elements/Time-Scale/#time-frame-toolbar)
    *   [Time frame customization](https://www.tradingview.com/charting-library-docs/v26/ui_elements/Time-Scale/#time-frame-customization)
        
    *   [Programmatic time frame setting](https://www.tradingview.com/charting-library-docs/v26/ui_elements/Time-Scale/#programmatic-time-frame-setting)
        
*   [Extended time scale](https://www.tradingview.com/charting-library-docs/v26/ui_elements/Time-Scale/#extended-time-scale)
    
*   [Extended sessions](https://www.tradingview.com/charting-library-docs/v26/ui_elements/Time-Scale/#extended-sessions)

---

# Price scale | Advanced Charts Documentation

[Skip to main content](https://www.tradingview.com/charting-library-docs/v27/ui_elements/Price-Scale/#__docusaurus_skipToContent_fallback)

This is documentation for **v27**, which is no longer actively maintained.

For up-to-date documentation, see the **[latest version](https://www.tradingview.com/charting-library-docs/)
**.

On this page

**Price scale** (or price axis) is a vertical scale on the right or left side of the chart that maps prices to coordinates and vice versa.

![Price scale](https://www.tradingview.com/charting-library-docs/v27/assets/images/price-scale-2df66cd5c629858b0eff237d4d973796.png)

UI interactions[​](https://www.tradingview.com/charting-library-docs/v27/ui_elements/Price-Scale/#ui-interactions "Direct link to UI interactions")

----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

This section describes how users can interact with the price scale.

### Adding price scale[​](https://www.tradingview.com/charting-library-docs/v27/ui_elements/Price-Scale/#adding-price-scale "Direct link to Adding price scale")

Users can add new price scales via the [_Compare symbol_](https://www.tradingview.com/charting-library-docs/v27/ui_elements/indicators/#add-and-compare-new-series)
 dialog by selecting _New price scale_.

![Add new price scale](https://www.tradingview.com/charting-library-docs/v27/assets/images/add-new-price-scale-e41df4d21863740fd1986c1c84071b4a.gif)

info

The library allows users to have up to eight price scales on the chart. However, only one price scale can be displayed on [mobile applications](https://www.tradingview.com/charting-library-docs/v27/mobile_specifics/)
.

### Changing position[​](https://www.tradingview.com/charting-library-docs/v27/ui_elements/Price-Scale/#changing-position "Direct link to Changing position")

Users can place the price scale on the left or right side of the chart. To do this, they should select the _Move scale to left/right_ button in the context menu of the price scale.

![Change price scale position](https://www.tradingview.com/charting-library-docs/v27/assets/images/move-scale-to-left-5f5a2139dd120390a736f6f02c29a4a7.gif)

You can also programmatically [change a price scale position](https://www.tradingview.com/charting-library-docs/v27/ui_elements/Price-Scale/#change-price-scale-position)
.

### Pinning to scale[​](https://www.tradingview.com/charting-library-docs/v27/ui_elements/Price-Scale/#pinning-to-scale "Direct link to Pinning to scale")

Users can select _Pin to scale_ in the symbol/indicator settings to attach symbols/indicators to the price scale.

![Pin to scale](https://www.tradingview.com/charting-library-docs/v27/assets/images/pin-to-scale-824f25927e7ad9bb7c8d0b56386d83df.gif)

You can also programmatically add a new indicator and [attach it to a new price scale](https://www.tradingview.com/charting-library-docs/v27/ui_elements/Price-Scale/#attach-indicators-to-price-scale)
.

### Inverting price scale[​](https://www.tradingview.com/charting-library-docs/v27/ui_elements/Price-Scale/#inverting-price-scale "Direct link to Inverting price scale")

Users can invert the scale via _Invert scale_ in the context menu of the price scale. When the option is enabled, the price increase is shown from top to bottom.

![Invert price scale](https://www.tradingview.com/charting-library-docs/v27/assets/images/invert-price-scale-ea2f11732ad2e9a5044a83279a294720.gif)

### Resetting price scale[​](https://www.tradingview.com/charting-library-docs/v27/ui_elements/Price-Scale/#resetting-price-scale "Direct link to Resetting price scale")

Users can reset the price scale when it is in a non-default state. Note that the _Reset price scale_ option will not appear in the context menu with adjustments such as moving the scale to the left or switching to logarithmic mode.

![Reset price scale](https://www.tradingview.com/charting-library-docs/v27/assets/images/reset-price-scale-ffca478582b6c29efe01996e61b6f57a.gif)

Style and default settings[​](https://www.tradingview.com/charting-library-docs/v27/ui_elements/Price-Scale/#style-and-default-settings "Direct link to Style and default settings")

-------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

### Customize price scale appearance[​](https://www.tradingview.com/charting-library-docs/v27/ui_elements/Price-Scale/#customize-price-scale-appearance "Direct link to Customize price scale appearance")

You can customize the appearance of the price scale using the [Overrides API](https://www.tradingview.com/charting-library-docs/v27/customization/overrides/)
. For example, the code sample below changes the text color and font size of the price and [time](https://www.tradingview.com/charting-library-docs/v27/ui_elements/Time-Scale)
 scales.

    const datafeed = new Datafeeds.UDFCompatibleDatafeed("https://demo-feed-data.tradingview.com");new TradingView.widget({    container: "chartContainer",    locale: "en",    library_path: "charting_library/",    datafeed: datafeed,    symbol: "AAPL",    interval: "1D",    overrides: {        "scalesProperties.textColor": "#FF0000",        "scalesProperties.fontSize": 14,    }})

Refer to the [Scale colors and fonts](https://www.tradingview.com/charting-library-docs/v27/customization/overrides/chart-overrides#scale-colors-and-fonts)
 section to see the full list of the overrides properties.

### Change price scale position[​](https://www.tradingview.com/charting-library-docs/v27/ui_elements/Price-Scale/#change-price-scale-position "Direct link to Change price scale position")

You can change the position of the price scale attached to the main series. When creating a chart, use the [`priceScaleSelectionStrategyName`](https://www.tradingview.com/charting-library-docs/v27/api/interfaces/Charting_Library.ChartPropertiesOverrides#pricescaleselectionstrategyname)
 property of the [Overrides API](https://www.tradingview.com/charting-library-docs/v27/customization/overrides/)
. For example, the code sample below changes the price scale position to the left.

    const datafeed = new Datafeeds.UDFCompatibleDatafeed("https://demo-feed-data.tradingview.com");new TradingView.widget({    container: "chartContainer",    locale: "en",    library_path: "charting_library/",    datafeed: datafeed,    symbol: "AAPL",    interval: "1D",    overrides: {        "priceScaleSelectionStrategyName": "left",    }})

If the chart is already created, use the [`changePriceScale`](https://www.tradingview.com/charting-library-docs/v27/api/interfaces/Charting_Library.ISeriesApi#changepricescale)
 method of the [`ISeriesApi`](https://www.tradingview.com/charting-library-docs/v27/api/interfaces/Charting_Library.ISeriesApi)
 interface. For example, the code sample below also changes the price scale position of the main series to the left.

    widget.onChartReady(() => {    widget.activeChart().getSeries().changePriceScale("new-left");});

### Attach indicators to price scale[​](https://www.tradingview.com/charting-library-docs/v27/ui_elements/Price-Scale/#attach-indicators-to-price-scale "Direct link to Attach indicators to price scale")

You can attach [indicators](https://www.tradingview.com/charting-library-docs/v27/ui_elements/indicators/)
 to the price scale. To do this, specify the [`priceScale`](https://www.tradingview.com/charting-library-docs/v27/api/interfaces/Charting_Library.CreateStudyOptions#pricescale)
 property in the `options` parameter of the [`createStudy`](https://www.tradingview.com/charting-library-docs/v27/api/interfaces/Charting_Library.IChartWidgetApi#createstudy)
 method. For example, the code sample below creates a _Volume_ indicator and attaches it to a new price scale on the left.

    widget.onChartReady(() => {    widget.activeChart().createStudy('Volume', false, false, undefined, undefined, { "priceScale": "new-left" });});

### Hide price scale[​](https://www.tradingview.com/charting-library-docs/v27/ui_elements/Price-Scale/#hide-price-scale "Direct link to Hide price scale")

If you want to hide the price scale when all series or indicators attached to it are hidden, enable the [`hide_price_scale_if_all_sources_hidden`](https://www.tradingview.com/charting-library-docs/v27/customization/Featuresets#hide_price_scale_if_all_sources_hidden)
 featureset.

### Hide settings button[​](https://www.tradingview.com/charting-library-docs/v27/ui_elements/Price-Scale/#hide-settings-button "Direct link to Hide settings button")

The scale settings button is available directly below the price scale. If users have more than one price scale on the chart, a letter will be displayed in place of the settings button. Users should click the letter that indicates the scale to open the corresponding scale settings. If you want to hide the settings button, disable the [`main_series_scale_menu`](https://www.tradingview.com/charting-library-docs/v27/customization/Featuresets#main_series_scale_menu)
 featureset.

### Add labels[​](https://www.tradingview.com/charting-library-docs/v27/ui_elements/Price-Scale/#add-labels "Direct link to Add labels")

You can add labels to the price scale to display additional information like a symbol name, bid/ask prices, indicators, and more. For example, the code sample below adds a symbol name label on the price scale.

    const datafeed = new Datafeeds.UDFCompatibleDatafeed("https://demo-feed-data.tradingview.com");new TradingView.widget({    container: "chartContainer",    locale: "en",    library_path: "charting_library/",    datafeed: datafeed,    symbol: "AAPL",    interval: "1D",    overrides: {        "scalesProperties.showSymbolLabels": true,    }})

Refer to the [Labels on the scale](https://www.tradingview.com/charting-library-docs/v27/customization/overrides/chart-overrides#labels-on-the-scale)
 section to see the full list of the overrides properties.

### Enable auto scaling[​](https://www.tradingview.com/charting-library-docs/v27/ui_elements/Price-Scale/#enable-auto-scaling "Direct link to Enable auto scaling")

You can enable auto scaling using the [`setAutoScale`](https://www.tradingview.com/charting-library-docs/v27/api/interfaces/Charting_Library.IPriceScaleApi#setautoscale)
 method of the [Price Scale API](https://www.tradingview.com/charting-library-docs/v27/api/interfaces/Charting_Library.IPriceScaleApi)
.

    widget.onChartReady(() => {    const priceScale = widget.activeChart().getPanes()[0].getMainSourcePriceScale();    priceScale.setAutoScale(true)});

### Set mode[​](https://www.tradingview.com/charting-library-docs/v27/ui_elements/Price-Scale/#set-mode "Direct link to Set mode")

You can set the price scale mode using the [Overrides API](https://www.tradingview.com/charting-library-docs/v27/customization/overrides/)
 or [Price Scale API](https://www.tradingview.com/charting-library-docs/v27/api/interfaces/Charting_Library.IPriceScaleApi)
. However, you cannot change the price scale mode using the [`applyOverrides`](https://www.tradingview.com/charting-library-docs/v27/api/interfaces/Charting_Library.IChartingLibraryWidget#applyoverrides)
 method.

When creating a chart, use [`overrides`](https://www.tradingview.com/charting-library-docs/v27/api/interfaces/Charting_Library.ChartingLibraryWidgetOptions#overrides)
 to change the price scale mode. For example, the code sample below enables the percentage mode.

    const datafeed = new Datafeeds.UDFCompatibleDatafeed("https://demo-feed-data.tradingview.com");new TradingView.widget({    container: "chartContainer",    locale: "en",    library_path: "charting_library/",    datafeed: datafeed,    symbol: "AAPL",    interval: "1D",    overrides: {        "mainSeriesProperties.priceAxisProperties.percentage": true,    }})

Refer to the [Price scale mode](https://www.tradingview.com/charting-library-docs/v27/customization/overrides/chart-overrides#price-scale-mode)
 section to see the full list of the overrides properties.

If the chart is already created, you can change the price scale mode only using the [`setMode`](https://www.tradingview.com/charting-library-docs/v27/api/interfaces/Charting_Library.IPriceScaleApi#setmode)
 method of the [Price Scale API](https://www.tradingview.com/charting-library-docs/v27/api/interfaces/Charting_Library.IPriceScaleApi)
. For example, the code sample below also enables the percentage mode.

    widget.onChartReady(() => {    const priceScale = widget.activeChart().getPanes()[0].getRightPriceScales()[0];    priceScale.setMode(2);});

### Set visible price range[​](https://www.tradingview.com/charting-library-docs/v27/ui_elements/Price-Scale/#set-visible-price-range "Direct link to Set visible price range")

If you want to set a visible price range of the price scale, you can use the [`setVisiblePriceRange`](https://www.tradingview.com/charting-library-docs/v27/api/interfaces/Charting_Library.IPriceScaleApi#setvisiblepricerange)
 method of the [Price Scale API](https://www.tradingview.com/charting-library-docs/v27/api/interfaces/Charting_Library.IPriceScaleApi)
. For example, the code sample below sets the visible price range between 140 and 170.

    widget.onChartReady(() => {    const priceScale = widget.activeChart().getPanes()[0].getRightPriceScales()[0];    priceScale.setVisiblePriceRange({ "from": 140, "to": 170 });});

Price formatting[​](https://www.tradingview.com/charting-library-docs/v27/ui_elements/Price-Scale/#price-formatting "Direct link to Price formatting")

-------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

The [`format`](https://www.tradingview.com/charting-library-docs/v27/api/interfaces/Charting_Library.LibrarySymbolInfo#format)
 property in the [`LibrarySymbolInfo`](https://www.tradingview.com/charting-library-docs/v27/api/interfaces/Charting_Library.LibrarySymbolInfo)
 object determines how prices appear on the scale. Specifying the `volume` format displays prices as decimals in thousands, millions, billions, or trillions.

If you specify the `price` format, the prices will be formatted in decimal or fractional format according to the `minmov`, `pricescale`, `minmove2`, `fractional`, `variablemintick` properties. Refer to the [Price Format](https://www.tradingview.com/charting-library-docs/v27/connecting_data/Symbology#price-format)
 section for more information.

You can also [change the decimal sign](https://www.tradingview.com/charting-library-docs/v27/ui_elements/Price-Scale/#change-decimal-sign)
 for the prices or [implement your custom logic](https://www.tradingview.com/charting-library-docs/v27/ui_elements/Price-Scale/#use-custom-formatting)
 of the price formatting.

### Change decimal sign[​](https://www.tradingview.com/charting-library-docs/v27/ui_elements/Price-Scale/#change-decimal-sign "Direct link to Change decimal sign")

You can apply custom formatting using the [`numeric_formatting`](https://www.tradingview.com/charting-library-docs/v27/api/interfaces/Charting_Library.ChartingLibraryWidgetOptions#numeric_formatting)
 property of the [Widget Constructor](https://www.tradingview.com/charting-library-docs/v27/core_concepts/Widget-Constructor)
. For example, the code sample below sets a comma to separate the decimal/fractional part of the prices.

    const datafeed = new Datafeeds.UDFCompatibleDatafeed("https://demo-feed-data.tradingview.com");new TradingView.widget({    container: "chartContainer",    locale: "en",    library_path: "charting_library/",    datafeed: datafeed,    symbol: "AAPL",    interval: "1D",    numeric_formatting: { decimal_sign: "," },})

The image below shows that the decimal parts of the prices on the price scale and the legend are separated with a comma.

![Numeric formatting](https://www.tradingview.com/charting-library-docs/v27/assets/images/price-scale-numeric-formatting-da8bbd44b24de8cb585575b3cecec1e4.png)

### Use custom formatting[​](https://www.tradingview.com/charting-library-docs/v27/ui_elements/Price-Scale/#use-custom-formatting "Direct link to Use custom formatting")

You can adjust the display format of price values using the [`custom_formatters`](https://www.tradingview.com/charting-library-docs/v27/api/interfaces/Charting_Library.ChartingLibraryWidgetOptions#custom_formatters)
 property of the [Widget Constructor](https://www.tradingview.com/charting-library-docs/v27/core_concepts/Widget-Constructor)
. For example, the code sample below defines a custom price formatting function for the `volume` [format type](https://www.tradingview.com/charting-library-docs/v27/api/interfaces/Charting_Library.LibrarySymbolInfo#format)
. This function replaces numerical zeros with letter representations like billions (B), millions (M), and thousands (K).

    const datafeed = new Datafeeds.UDFCompatibleDatafeed("https://demo-feed-data.tradingview.com");new TradingView.widget({    container: "chartContainer",    locale: "en",    library_path: "charting_library/",    datafeed: datafeed,    symbol: "AAPL",    interval: "1D",    custom_formatters: {        priceFormatterFactory: (symbolInfo, minTick) => {            if (symbolInfo === null) {                return null;            }            if (symbolInfo.format === 'volume') {                return {                    format: (price, signPositive) => {                        if (price >= 1000000000) {                            return `${(price / 1000000000).toFixed(3)}B`;                        }                        if (price >= 1000000) {                            return `${(price / 1000000).toFixed(3)}M`;                        }                        if (price >= 1000) {                            return `${(price / 1000).toFixed(3)}K`;                        }                        return price.toFixed(2);                    },                };            }            return null; // The default formatter will be used.        },    }})

Symbol currency[​](https://www.tradingview.com/charting-library-docs/v27/ui_elements/Price-Scale/#symbol-currency "Direct link to Symbol currency")

----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

### Display symbol currency[​](https://www.tradingview.com/charting-library-docs/v27/ui_elements/Price-Scale/#display-symbol-currency "Direct link to Display symbol currency")

To display symbol currency on the price scale, follow the steps below.

1.  Enable the [`pricescale_currency`](https://www.tradingview.com/charting-library-docs/v27/customization/Featuresets#pricescale_currency)
     featureset to show the _Currency_ menu in _Chart Settings → Scales_.
2.  Assign a currency code to the [`currency_code`](https://www.tradingview.com/charting-library-docs/v27/api/interfaces/Charting_Library.LibrarySymbolInfo#currency_code)
     property of the [`LibrarySymbolInfo`](https://www.tradingview.com/charting-library-docs/v27/api/interfaces/Charting_Library.LibrarySymbolInfo)
     object. Note that the currency code must be a three-letter string in the [ISO 4217](https://www.iso.org/iso-4217-currency-codes.html)
     format.

### Enable currency conversion[​](https://www.tradingview.com/charting-library-docs/v27/ui_elements/Price-Scale/#enable-currency-conversion "Direct link to Enable currency conversion")

The library provides a drop-down menu for selecting the currency in which the symbol is displayed. To switch between currencies, you should implement the currency conversion. Follow the steps below to enable the currency conversion.

1.  Enable the [`pricescale_currency`](https://www.tradingview.com/charting-library-docs/v27/customization/Featuresets#pricescale_currency)
     featureset to show the _Currency_ menu in _Chart Settings → Scales_.
2.  Add the [`currency_code`](https://www.tradingview.com/charting-library-docs/v27/api/interfaces/Charting_Library.LibrarySymbolInfo#currency_code)
     and [`original_currency_code`](https://www.tradingview.com/charting-library-docs/v27/api/interfaces/Charting_Library.LibrarySymbolInfo#original_currency_code)
     properties to the [`LibrarySymbolInfo`](https://www.tradingview.com/charting-library-docs/v27/api/interfaces/Charting_Library.LibrarySymbolInfo)
     object. Note that the currency code must be a three-letter string in the [ISO 4217](https://www.iso.org/iso-4217-currency-codes.html)
     format.
3.  Provide a list of available currencies in the [`currency_codes`](https://www.tradingview.com/charting-library-docs/v27/api/interfaces/Charting_Library.DatafeedConfiguration#currency_codes)
     property of the [`DatafeedConfiguration`](https://www.tradingview.com/charting-library-docs/v27/api/interfaces/Charting_Library.DatafeedConfiguration)
     object.
4.  Pass [`currencyCode`](https://www.tradingview.com/charting-library-docs/v27/api/interfaces/Charting_Library.SymbolResolveExtension#currencycode)
     to the [`resolveSymbol`](https://www.tradingview.com/charting-library-docs/v27/connecting_data/Datafeed-API#resolvesymbol)
     method.
5.  Implement the conversion algorithm. The conversion algorithms can vary, but you can choose one from the following two:
    *   **Constant currency rate algorithm**. With this approach, you retrieve the current currency exchange rate and multiply each bar by this value. However, this approach may yield inaccurate results for historical bars if the exchange rate has fluctuated significantly over time.
    *   **Corresponding currency rate algorithm**. With this approach, you request historical currency exchange rates from the server and then multiply each bar by its corresponding currency rate.

info

Implementing either of these algorithms is straightforward when all symbols and Forex rates share the same time zone. However, if time zones differ, you need to find an appropriate way to accurately match the corresponding rate to each bar.

After this, users can view symbol prices in their preferred currency. To switch to a different currency, users can click the currency drop‑down menu and choose another option. When a new currency is selected, the `resolveSymbol` method is called with the same `symbolInfo` but with an added `currency_code` property indicating the chosen currency.

![Hide context menu](https://www.tradingview.com/charting-library-docs/v27/assets/images/currency-drop-down-menu-b61960f1893b731987c23c7fae9e2dfb.png)

*   [UI interactions](https://www.tradingview.com/charting-library-docs/v27/ui_elements/Price-Scale/#ui-interactions)
